														- Senior Manager, Marketing Automation
															- We’ve built a community of millions of readers and hundreds of thousands of subscribers interested in self development. As we grow, we’d like the touchpoints with our users to become increasingly personal. By knowing their interests and goals, and pairing that with behavioral data, we can provide them the best material to live a Rich Life.
															- Our ideal candidate is an expert in getting the most out of marketing automation systems. You should be an analytical and operational thinker. You’re familiar with building automation systems and recommending a strategic roadmap of personalization at different levels of scale.
															- In this role, you will advise on our marketing automation strategies and maintain a rock-solid operating infrastructure for our leads, customer database, reporting and analytics.
															- As Senior Manager, Marketing Automation, you’ll lead our Marketing Automation team. This team manages our millions of monthly emails, email production, campaign execution and testing, marketing automation,and CRM activities. They also perform a range of operational and analytical support across our demand-generation initiatives and customer interactions.
															- What You’ll Be Doing
																- Lead the research, selection, implementation and day to day operation of our marketing automation toolset for capturing, scoring, nurturing and tracking customer prospects and maintaining customer and transactions data.
																- Function as our internal expert for our marketing and sales automation technologies, currently, InfusionSoft and Maropost.
																- Build and manage key integrations - web site tools, analytics applications, customer support system - working closely with vendors and internal teams
																- Collaborate with our content marketing and growth teams to build, execute, automate and measure the effectiveness of email marketing campaigns and funnels.
																- Build and execute comprehensive testing plans, including subject lines, offer, copy, etc. Create and maintain internal best practices guide based on lessons learned.
																- Run hundreds of digital experiments, monitor email deliverability and maintain a high rate of deliverability.
																- Design quality control procedures to ensure email production and campaigns are executed flawlessly.
																- Create new automated ways to measure, profile, and segment audiences and build sound nurture programs with the goal to continuously maximize marketing conversions.
																- Develop, implement and enforce policies and procedures to ensure data integrity and cleanliness and efficiency of databases. Document processes and develop training for all users to ensure adherence to processes.
																- Perform analysis and build dashboards to report on email and campaign performance, leads and other attributes and performance metrics
																- Vet out new marketing technology solutions to build the infrastructure to advance our marketing capabilities and operational efficiencies.
															- Desired Skills and Experience
																- B2C marketing background with 5+ years experience in marketing automation, campaign management, leads processing (including lead scoring), marketing/CRM database, preferably in a high volume environment
																- 2+ years experience in managing and developing direct reports and building high performing teams
																- Successful track record of implementing and managing marketing automation systems and associated integrations including CRM integration, field and database management, custom objects, campaign management and configuration, lead scoring, reporting. Hands-on experience working with leading marketing and CRM systems . Must be familiar with marketing automation tool landscape.
																- Working knowledge of HTML, Javascript, and CSS strongly preferred
																- Demonstrated proficiency with database design concepts, conditional logic and segmentation
																- Excellent at planning and project management – ability to look ahead to meet regular deadlines and predict and prevent last minute fire drills.
																- Must be resourceful and have keen problem solving skills through a combination of research and consulting with experts
																- Must have excellent written and verbal communications skills with ability to effectively interact with both technical and non-technical staff
																- Strong analytic and quantitative skills - ability to use data to develop and measure marketing programs
															- Education and Other Qualifications
																- BA/BS Degree required or equivalent combination of experience and education (preferred areas of study: Marketing, Computer Science, Statistics or other related fields)
																- Technical degree/background a plus
																- Prior experience with InfusionSoft or in managing migrations of leading tools such as Marketo, Salesforce a strong plus
																- Self-starter and highly organized with exceptional attention to detail
																- Must thrive in a fast paced, iterative, deadline driven environment and be able to manage priorities and respond to challenges under pressure
																- Able to work remotely (with your own phone, laptop and high speed internet access in place)

													- Growth Engineer
														- The Giant Hairy Problem You’ll Be Solving
															- We’ve got some pretty aggressive goals for growing our business and we’re building a fully functional team to drive growth forward. Engineers, marketers, and designers all the same team with a single goal in mind: drive as much growth in as short of a time as possible.
															- We already have a world-class team in place. But how much faster can we go? 10X? 100X? This is where you come in. You’ll work with us to find every possible source of growth.
															- Standard marketing strategies aren't going to cut it. We need to blend marketing with engineering in order to scale our funnels by an order of magnitude. We aggressively A/B test all of our ideas, try every hack we can think of for scaling acquisition, and wrestle with untested channels that no one else has tapped yet.
															- The caliber of talent on this team is high. You’ll be pushed to your limits and on the cutting edge.
															- You’ll Learn More In 3 Months Than You Did All Of Last Year
															- We’re not here to follow mediocre best practices. We want to deeply understand what drives a business forward. Which variables have all the leverage? How do we persuade people to take action? How can we scale beyond our wildest expectations?
															- We have a simple goal on the Growth team: learn faster than anyone else in the industry. The faster we learn, the faster we grow the company. You’ll be right in the thick of it. There’s no ego on this team so your ideas will have a chance to prove themselves just like everyone else. We force ourselves to constantly evolve our understanding of what actually drives growth.
															- We reach this depth of understanding by operating at an incredibly high tempo. Our iteration and feedback loops happen at a blistering pace. Testing combined with an intense tempo means you’ll learn faster than any of your peers.
														- You'll spend your time:
															- Running back-to-back A/B tests on every part of our business.
															- Building the front-end for campaigns that bend the internet to our will.
															- Experimenting with freemium tools and viral loops.
															- Finding ways to hack channels to acquire traffic and users in ways no one has thought of before.
															- Building internal tools and infrastructure for our data and testing.
														- And you'll be a great fit if:
															- You’re really strong on the front-end (HTML, CSS)
															- You’re also pretty good at JavaScript and PHP.
															- Growth projects are HIGHLY iterative. Many ideas won’t work. This means you’ll put your heart and soul into projects that flop on a regular basis. That’s the nature of the work. But you need to be able to tackle the next iteration with as much gusto and passion as you did the first time. Out of 10 of our projects, at least 8 of them won't work. You're okay with that.
															- Our team moves at an exceptionally high tempo, you’ll be shipping weekly if not daily. You’ll need to find that perfect balance between high quality code and getting a project shipped.
															- You ship. And you always meet deadlines.
															- You obsess over your results and are always connecting your work back to business goals like revenue and profit.
															- This is a 100% remote position, work from anywhere you like.
														- If you think you’d be a great addition to our team, click the “Apply Now” button below to submit your application.

															- ♥

													- Project managers
														- manage projects and employees, essential for expansion
														- Are you ready to live a rich life? Are you looking for your dream job? If those two thoughts resonate with you then this might be the company and the role for you!
														- I Will Teach You to Be Rich is a widely-recognized company focused on personal finance and entrepreneurship for college students, recent college grades and everyone else. Founded in 2004 by Ramit Sethi, author of the New York Times best selling book of the same name, our community now hosts over 250,000 readers per month and 100,000+ newsletter subscribers. We’ve been featured in other major media, including the Wall Street Journal, New York Times, NPR, ABC News, and CNBC.
														- The company’s mission is to inspire people and provide them with tested strategies and tools to help them achieve their goals. Through our acclaimed content, products and courses, we show people how to use psychology, persuasion, and behavioral change to improve their own lives and the ones around them.
														- We are looking for an outstanding project manager to join our team and lead and manage our company growth endeavors. You’ll be at the center of our mission critical projects and initiatives, providing guidance and direction to team members, setting priorities, and interfacing with senior leadership.
														- Job Description
															- The Senior Project Manager has responsibility for managing and ensuring the successful execution of the company’s major launches and other strategic projects.  This is a key member of our team, specifically focused on balancing our pursuit of driving efficiencies with our commitment to deliver world-class products and services for our customers.  We are looking for an experienced, focused and self-directed professional who can oversee multiple, concurrent projects and facilitate all phases of project development, including planning, creative and technology development, production/implementation, testing and evaluation.
															- The ideal candidate will be well versed in project management methodologies and tools, have a rich background of project successes, be energetic and personable, have a can-do attitude and an ability to thrive in a dynamic environment.  He/she will possess a blend of business and technology savvy and be comfortable working with cross-functional teams and coordinating activities involving product design and development, content development, technology and systems, email and online marketing, customer support, and finance.
															- The Senior Project Manager will provide leadership in helping us develop our processes and systems to better support our product launches, as well as create the structures and stability necessary to sustain growth into the future.
															- This position is full time and virtual/home-based.
														- Key Responsibilities:
															- The Senior Project Manager provides day-to-day project leadership for the company’s major launches and strategic projects and is accountable for achieving objectives relating to project scope, quality, timing and budget.
															- Translate business objectives and project requirements into accurate and detailed project plans and timelines and drive cross-functional teams in achievement of project deliverables.
															- Oversee the creation and execution of detailed work breakdown/project plans and ensure that projects are executed with exceptional attention to detail and follow-through.
															- Manage the operational aspects of major projects, including planning and facilitating regular team meetings and working sessions, creating and communicating agendas, capturing and disseminating meeting notes and next steps, assigning tasks and creating deadlines for each task.
															- Work closely with product development, designers, technical leads, media producers, copywriters and operations personnel to coordinate their efforts and assist in managing their priorities.
															- Manage shifting and competing priorities and resolve conflicts involving scheduling, resources or technical issues.
															- Proactively identify and mitigate project issues and risks.
															- Serve as escalation point for issues including issue resolution involving senior management.
															- Creatively solve problems and bottlenecks to keep projects on track
															- Regularly communicate status of projects and issues to team members and key stakeholders.
															- Provide leadership in the creation and documentation of IWT’s project management processes and practices.
															- Contribute to the ongoing development of the organization’s processes, workflows and new initiatives.  Play an active role in evaluating current processes and recommending and implementing continual improvements.
															- Establish and foster relationships across the organization, and use these to gain consensus, create and communicate plans, changes and conflict resolution.
															- Must be comfortable managing teams in a matrixed environment where you’ll be responsible for deadlines and keeping projects on track, but not have full authority over functional members time.
															- Cultivate a spirit of supportive, constructive team interactions in the service of delivering high quality results.
															- Support CEO in strategic and financial analysis and planning.
															- Prepare launch metric reports, debriefs, progress reports and presentations for senior management regarding overall operations and specific project status and budgets.
														- Requirements:
															- 5+ years experience in project management of B2C products, services or events.
															- Excellent organizational skills, project tracking abilities, strong attention to detail
															- Rich background of project successes and experience in managing complex projects from conception to implementation, including defining and communicating specifications, managing resources and leading cross-functional teams, developing and maintaining detailed project schedules and milestones based on business objectives and timing requirements.
															- Able to effectively prioritize and execute tasks and work with a sense of urgency often under time constraints and pressure to deliver while remaining the calm in the storm.
															- Ability to manage multiple projects simultaneously in various phases
															- Demonstrated ability to document process workflows for complex processes
															- Previous experience and success in managing a remote team
															- Understanding of B2C internet marketing and information product launch processes and lifecycle, content management systems, email marketing and E-commerce platforms, and web analytics.
															- Strong analytical and problem-solving skills
															- Excellent interpersonal and communication skills and proven ability to work effectively with all levels of an organization including senior executives
															- Self-starter with excellent time management skills and capability of managing high priority projects with little supervision.
															- Be prepared to push back and deny feature requests that are out of scope while simultaneously offering senior management options on how to evaluate tradeoffs in quality, time, and scope.
															- Willingness to ask questions for more clarity when needed
														- Other Requirements:
															- BA or BS degree
															- Project management certification (e.g. PMP) a plus
															- Web development experience a plus.
															- Strong working knowledge of Basecamp, Google Docs Smartsheets and Trello and/or other project management tools
															- Working knowledge of shopping cart systems (InfusionSoft), analytics tools such as Tableau or Graphly
															- Excellent Powerpoint, Excel and Word skills.
															- Skillful at working virtually while fostering a team environment.
															- Comfortable with ambiguity. We’re a small company and priorities shift, sometimes rapidly.
														- If you think you’d be a great addition to our team, click the “Apply Now” button below to submit your application.
												- Hiring an Intern
													- Here’s the broad strokes– my friend Sean owns a small resort in the Philippines. He needs marketing and technical support, and enjoys having somebody smart around to bounce ideas off of. In return for about 2 hours a day of your effort, he compensates you with a free room, laundry service, and 3 meals a day. The value works out to be about $15 bucks an hour. It’s a relatively stress free way to spend almost nothing for 6 months or more.
													- The gig is designed to be a soft landing into the digital nomad lifestyle (or an extension for those on the trail). A handful of the people who’ve done this report they spent less than $50 a month (mostly in Visa fees), which means you could realistically go 6 months and only spend $300.
													- Instead of blabbing on, I’ll let Grant, the guy currently at the resort, do the talking. I’ll come back later in the post to clarify the fine print.
													- “This is a chance to instantly transition into a traveling entrepreneur, but with a massive safety net for 6+ months. It’s really the perfect transition you could ask for. You can employ a sole focus in developing whatever business you want while awesome people take care of you. It allows you to work hard and take risks…because if a venture fails after a month…you still have a sweet lifestyle that you are paying 0 dollars for. And your “boss” (Sean is much more a bro than a boss, best exemplified by our weekly MMA classes where we end up sparring each other) has a vested interest in your success, which equals tons of support and encouragement.
													- Anyone who is wanting to take the leap abroad, or is currently traveling but not getting anything done, this position gives you the stability and ideal environment to get done in about 6 months what would take years. Consider it your 6 month, real-life Tropical MBA. Sorry, no diploma.
													- Want some numbers? Full disclosure: I came here with income exactly equal to my living expenses and 300 dollars in my bank account (literally). By the time I leave, my income will have grown by roughly 400%, with savings up 2,000%. The majority of all of this comes from a business I started while here.”.
													- Just imagine how creative you can be without any distractions– no cooking, cleaning, laundry, commute, shopping, bills, or odd jobs. You’ll be (quite literally) waited on every day.
													- What’s the catch? Well it’s not all just fun and games. You’ll need to be adept at building partnerships with groups, clubs, and travel agents– both overseas and domestic– that will stay at the resorts. This could include, but is not limited to, scuba diving clubs, corporate parties, conferences, weddings, reunions, etc. The expectation is that you put in 2 productive hours of work each day, or 14 hours each week. This work should be spread evenly over the week and not batched in one or two days, as there are some day to day tasks that need carried out (like affiliate relationships with Agoda, etc).
													- Duties include:
														- Executing a sales and marketing plan to attract more group bookings to the resort.
														- General tech support around the resorts, including WordPress websites.
														- Update & SEO websites, Sean’s YouTube channel, etc
														- Continue ongoing marketing campaigns.
														- Maintain and update existing booking agencies (Agoda, Trip Advisor, and similar).
														- Update social media sites.
													- We are looking for helpful team players, willing to integrate into the business and be part of the Badladz crew. Everyone in Puerto Galera will get to know you and you’ll become part of the family here.
													- General expectations:
														- You are results focused.
														- You’ve got no problem with tech and marketing stuff– WordPress, content marketing, social media, SEO, Aweber, affiliate programs and all the rest.
														- You have excellent WordPress skills. The whole business runs on WordPress, so we are looking for people with a demonstrated track record with the platform.
														- You are excellent with computers in general.
														- You know functional SEO for websites and YouTube videos (Sean’s videos have over 1 Million views!)
														- The Philippines is a crazy developing country, candidates will do well to address the “fit” issue and let us know why they are drawn to live on a remote island in the Philippines (or show some kind of experience or drive to do so). It’s probably worth listening to my interview with Sean and checking out his videos.
													- Caveats:
														- Although they’ve got a kick-ass restaurant, pool, and world-class view, the accommodations at the resort are small and basic. If you aren’t used to living in “traveler” level accommodations, this isn’t the place for you to stay.
														- People who love Puerto Galera love it for its “wild west” vibe. This place is way off the trail. Quiet beaches that on other islands would have 5-star resorts, weird dudes in quiet salons drinking at 10AM, locals hanging around haggling you for trike rides, etc. Successful candidates in the past have demonstrated that they are not only prepared for the craziness that is the Philippines, but that they appreciate it.
														- The WIFI is slow and can be spotty– the power goes out frequently and when it does there is no WIFI (although you can fire up the 3G routers). If your business depends on dependable and fast WIFI, this isn’t the gig for you. That said, you can Skype and watch Youtube, but you just have to be patient.
														- Historically this has been a win/win situation for candidates who have a generous, patient, team oriented personality and attitude. If you count hours or get easily perturbed, it’s best not to apply to this position. The resort is a family run business not a high tech firm. (As a concrete example of this– say the resort is really busy due to Holy Week, and Mylene keeps asking you to switch rooms to better accommodate paying guests. Honestly ask yourself, do you roll with it and think it’s cool the resort is having a great week, or do you roll your eyes and grumble?) Nothing wrong with the latter, it’s just not a good fit for this environment.
														- Sean will be around quite a bit, DCers and other interesting folks pop in occasionally, but the reality of this position is that most of the time you are going to be the only internet marketer on the island. That can be an isolating experience for some. I don’t want to give the impression that there will be like-minded entrepreneurs popping out of the wood work on a weekly basis. That said, I think that all the previous candidates would agree that they’ve had significant networking opportunities as a direct result of this gig, it just isn’t a daily thing, and often happens online and through the broader DC community.
														- Just so you know, I am hardly involved in this beyond posting this job ad. I swing through occasionally, and I talk to Sean regularly, but I’m not really involved.
														- We are looking to make a deal with an individual, so please no couples or family applications.
														- Some application advice here: “How to Get Your Dream Job”
													- From Jeff Parke:
													- “This is one of the best opportunities in the location independent entrepreneurial universe.
													- This is not an exaggeration, the day I applied to the TMBA internship was one of the most important days of my life.  Soon I was hopping a flight to the other side of the planet and accelerating toward the lifestyle I’d been clawing after.
													- The beauty of this internship is that you have an unbelievable amount of time on your hands to do whatever-the-hell-you-want.  For me that has been tapping away on the laptop, making new connections, and bootstrapping an online biznass.  Back in the States, I tried to make it happen on nights and weekends as I suffered through the corporate day-job grind.
													- Here, you can truly reclaim your time and energy, and use it to wrangle whatever you’re dreaming up.
													- Speaking of dreaming, I still have to pinch myself sometimes as I look up from my laptop and gaze out at the turquoise waters and green islands in the distance.
													- Mylene, Sean, and the staff at Badladz are all really great people and will take care of just about anything you need.  I’ve had a ton of fun here and recommend it to anyone who wants to take their business/lifestyle to the next level.”
													- Ben Comment
													- Screen-Shot-2012-08-14-at-8.52.19-PM1
													- Logistics:
														- The compensation for this job is a free room, 3 daily meals (delicious!), coffee/tea, and free laundry for 6 months.
														- Application deadline is 11:59 PM September 5th, 2014 EST.
														- We’ll try to make a decision by September 10th.
														- We’d love for you to start October 25th, 2014. (Early or late starts are okay as well, main criteria is skill set and quality application). Keep in mind that all 3 of us will be at DCBKK in Bangkok on October 16th, so if you are in BKK (or want to be) it would be a great time to meet and hang out as well as get a feel for the broader DC community.
														- The deal extends for 6 months. For people who do great work and want to stay longer, Sean has historically been willing to extend the deal.
													- Here’s how you can apply:
														- Record a 1:00 video of yourself. Tell us why you’d be a great fit for Sean’s resort. Set the video as “unlisted” and grab the URL.
														- Fill out this form, and be sure to have your YouTube (or other video sharing service) link handy.
														- There is an optional field on the form to link to a website that shows other work you’ve done. This is not required.
														- That’s it!
													- If you have any questions about the offer, please ask them in the comments so I can answer them for everyone’s benefit.
													- Here’s the broad strokes
														- my friend Sean owns a small resort in the Philippines. He needs marketing and technical support, and enjoys having somebody smart around to bounce ideas off of. In return for about 2 hours a day of your effort, he compensates you with a free room, laundry service, and 3 meals a day. The value works out to be about $15 bucks an hour. It’s a relatively stress free way to spend almost nothing for 6 months or more.
														- The gig is designed to be a soft landing into the digital nomad lifestyle (or an extension for those on the trail). A handful of the people who’ve done this report they spent less than $50 a month (mostly in Visa fees), which means you could realistically go 6 months and only spend $300.
														- Instead of blabbing on, I’ll let Grant, the guy currently at the resort, do the talking. I’ll come back later in the post to clarify the fine print.
														- “This is a chance to instantly transition into a traveling entrepreneur, but with a massive safety net for 6+ months. It’s really the perfect transition you could ask for. You can employ a sole focus in developing whatever business you want while awesome people take care of you. It allows you to work hard and take risks…because if a venture fails after a month…you still have a sweet lifestyle that you are paying 0 dollars for. And your “boss” (Sean is much more a bro than a boss, best exemplified by our weekly MMA classes where we end up sparring each other) has a vested interest in your success, which equals tons of support and encouragement.
														- Anyone who is wanting to take the leap abroad, or is currently traveling but not getting anything done, this position gives you the stability and ideal environment to get done in about 6 months what would take years. Consider it your 6 month, real-life Tropical MBA. Sorry, no diploma.
														- Want some numbers? Full disclosure: I came here with income exactly equal to my living expenses and 300 dollars in my bank account (literally). By the time I leave, my income will have grown by roughly 400%, with savings up 2,000%. The majority of all of this comes from a business I started while here.”.
														- Just imagine how creative you can be without any distractions– no cooking, cleaning, laundry, commute, shopping, bills, or odd jobs. You’ll be (quite literally) waited on every day.
														- What’s the catch? Well it’s not all just fun and games. You’ll need to be adept at building partnerships with groups, clubs, and travel agents– both overseas and domestic– that will stay at the resorts. This could include, but is not limited to, scuba diving clubs, corporate parties, conferences, weddings, reunions, etc. The expectation is that you put in 2 productive hours of work each day, or 14 hours each week. This work should be spread evenly over the week and not batched in one or two days, as there are some day to day tasks that need carried out (like affiliate relationships with Agoda, etc).
													- Duties include:
														- Executing a sales and marketing plan to attract more group bookings to the resort.
														- General tech support around the resorts, including WordPress websites.
														- Update & SEO websites, Sean’s YouTube channel, etc
														- Continue ongoing marketing campaigns.
														- Maintain and update existing booking agencies (Agoda, Trip Advisor, and similar).
														- Update social media sites.
													- We are looking for helpful team players, willing to integrate into the business and be part of the Badladz crew. Everyone in Puerto Galera will get to know you and you’ll become part of the family here.
													- General expectations:
														- You are results focused.
														- You’ve got no problem with tech and marketing stuff– WordPress, content marketing, social media, SEO, Aweber, affiliate programs and all the rest.
														- You have excellent WordPress skills. The whole business runs on WordPress, so we are looking for people with a demonstrated track record with the platform.
														- You are excellent with computers in general.
														- You know functional SEO for websites and YouTube videos (Sean’s videos have over 1 Million views!)
														- The Philippines is a crazy developing country, candidates will do well to address the “fit” issue and let us know why they are drawn to live on a remote island in the Philippines (or show some kind of experience or drive to do so). It’s probably worth listening to my interview with Sean and checking out his videos.
													- Caveats:
														- Although they’ve got a kick-ass restaurant, pool, and world-class view, the accommodations at the resort are small and basic. If you aren’t used to living in “traveler” level accommodations, this isn’t the place for you to stay.
														- People who love Puerto Galera love it for its “wild west” vibe. This place is way off the trail. Quiet beaches that on other islands would have 5-star resorts, weird dudes in quiet salons drinking at 10AM, locals hanging around haggling you for trike rides, etc. Successful candidates in the past have demonstrated that they are not only prepared for the craziness that is the Philippines, but that they appreciate it.
														- The WIFI is slow and can be spotty– the power goes out frequently and when it does there is no WIFI (although you can fire up the 3G routers). If your business depends on dependable and fast WIFI, this isn’t the gig for you. That said, you can Skype and watch Youtube, but you just have to be patient.
														- Historically this has been a win/win situation for candidates who have a generous, patient, team oriented personality and attitude. If you count hours or get easily perturbed, it’s best not to apply to this position. The resort is a family run business not a high tech firm. (As a concrete example of this– say the resort is really busy due to Holy Week, and Mylene keeps asking you to switch rooms to better accommodate paying guests. Honestly ask yourself, do you roll with it and think it’s cool the resort is having a great week, or do you roll your eyes and grumble?) Nothing wrong with the latter, it’s just not a good fit for this environment.
														- Sean will be around quite a bit, DCers and other interesting folks pop in occasionally, but the reality of this position is that most of the time you are going to be the only internet marketer on the island. That can be an isolating experience for some. I don’t want to give the impression that there will be like-minded entrepreneurs popping out of the wood work on a weekly basis. That said, I think that all the previous candidates would agree that they’ve had significant networking opportunities as a direct result of this gig, it just isn’t a daily thing, and often happens online and through the broader DC community.
														- Just so you know, I am hardly involved in this beyond posting this job ad. I swing through occasionally, and I talk to Sean regularly, but I’m not really involved.
														- We are looking to make a deal with an individual, so please no couples or family applications.
														- Some application advice here: “How to Get Your Dream Job”
													- From Jeff Parke:
														- “This is one of the best opportunities in the location independent entrepreneurial universe.
														- This is not an exaggeration, the day I applied to the TMBA internship was one of the most important days of my life.  Soon I was hopping a flight to the other side of the planet and accelerating toward the lifestyle I’d been clawing after.
														- The beauty of this internship is that you have an unbelievable amount of time on your hands to do whatever-the-hell-you-want.  For me that has been tapping away on the laptop, making new connections, and bootstrapping an online biznass.  Back in the States, I tried to make it happen on nights and weekends as I suffered through the corporate day-job grind.
														- Here, you can truly reclaim your time and energy, and use it to wrangle whatever you’re dreaming up.
														- Speaking of dreaming, I still have to pinch myself sometimes as I look up from my laptop and gaze out at the turquoise waters and green islands in the distance.
														- Mylene, Sean, and the staff at Badladz are all really great people and will take care of just about anything you need.  I’ve had a ton of fun here and recommend it to anyone who wants to take their business/lifestyle to the next level.”
													- Ben Comment
													- Screen-Shot-2012-08-14-at-8.52.19-PM1
													- Logistics:
														- The compensation for this job is a free room, 3 daily meals (delicious!), coffee/tea, and free laundry for 6 months.
														- Application deadline is 11:59 PM September 5th, 2014 EST.
														- We’ll try to make a decision by September 10th.
														- We’d love for you to start October 25th, 2014. (Early or late starts are okay as well, main criteria is skill set and quality application). Keep in mind that all 3 of us will be at DCBKK in Bangkok on October 16th, so if you are in BKK (or want to be) it would be a great time to meet and hang out as well as get a feel for the broader DC community.
														- The deal extends for 6 months. For people who do great work and want to stay longer, Sean has historically been willing to extend the deal.
													- Here’s how you can apply:
														- Record a 1:00 video of yourself. Tell us why you’d be a great fit for Sean’s resort. Set the video as “unlisted” and grab the URL.
														- Fill out this form, and be sure to have your YouTube (or other video sharing service) link handy.
														- There is an optional field on the form to link to a website that shows other work you’ve done. This is not required.
														- That’s it!
													- If you have any questions about the offer, please ask them in the comments so I can answer them for everyone’s benefit.

Find the need. Exploit their customer service gaffes. Is it impossible to receive good service? Do dissatisfied customers litter the Web with their displeasure?

										- advice
										  collapsed:: true
											- 3. Register every variation of keywords (what people would search for when wanting to find out about the process) as domain names. Be sure you buy the .com, .net, .org and .info domains for each high quality domain that you purchase. Failure to do so may leave your business more vulnerable to competition, and dilute your brand.
											- 5. Write instructions / a book / produce videos, etc... Detailing your new process.
											- 6. Write ten or more articles about the process. The more the better. You can also write articles that only pertain to the general industry. This content should not be pushing your product specifically, but should be written to only help the reader. This builds trust and enhances SEO. You also need a good sales page that sells your product specifically.
											- 7. Build a website that showcases your helpful content as a first priority, and advertises your product as a second priority in the information hierarchy of the website. A clean design, prioritizing content, is more important than graphics. Complicated graphics can actually distract and otherwise turn some people off to what you are offering. Make graphics as professional as possible, but use only where necessary.

									- Pros/Cons
										- Pros
										- Cons
											- Learning very niche knowledge (time consuming and isn't very transferable)
												- however same could be said of any Niche Site
											- each product may only last a while (low longevity thus time consuming). Basically a salesman
											- Paid traffic/lead generation can lead to constantly running new campaigns - not passive
											- - Time better spent owning the funnel by creating the product
									- _Choose a product_
										- The easiest way to make money online is to find a product that you use or like and then build a small niche site around it.
										- Find high commission affiliate programs
										  #AffiliatePrograms https://workflowy.com/#/db1d19acc662
										- _Criteria_
											- Choose products with high supply:demand ratios
												- Avoid high supply:low demand (overcrowded)
												- Avoid low demand
											- Dos and Donts
												- Don't focus on ebooks
												- Don't focus on cheap affiliate marketing (e.g. 90% of reviews being Amazon) or ads
									- Project Plan Progression
										- Write review posts with soft selling
											- Evergreen articles with a soft CTA
											- SEO is easy. In 99% of cases all you need to do is title a post “[Product’s name] Review” or “[Product’s name] By [Creator’s name]” and you’ll do alright

										- eCommerce Inventory-based/importing
										  collapsed:: true

										- Amazon FBA
										  collapsed:: true
											- Overview of pros and cons
												- Pros
													-
												- Cons
													- Small margins?
													- You can't sell your storefront, or your list. For us its great for monthly sales, but you also have to diversify, because you don't have much control over your distribution.
													- Various claims
														- Amazon doesn't let you remove an item once they're selling it, so if you want to setup your own store so you can sell the item exclusively yourself, Amazon will steal your customers so you don't get the email addresses of your potential customers.
														- Amazon doesn't let you remove and Amazon Store, so when people type the name of your store into Google, your "store" on the Amazon website will appear first even if you've removed all the products from your store.
														- When you sell items on an Amazon Store and get traction, Amazon will find out what you're selling, and sell the exact same item you're selling at a cheaper price undercutting you due to their buying power. Then you can't sell the product any more.
														- Amazon is notorious for either suspending people or banning them from a category if you outsell Amazon on a product they themselves sell. Essentially success equals failure on AMazon if they sell in your niche.
														- Whether Amazon or eBay its a huge audience and they drive tons of traffic but you have to putup with their rules, scammers, stupid buyers and other headaches. Everyone has to decide if its worth it for them.
											- Costs
												- Look at the lowest price an item is selling for. Subtract Amazon fees (15%, cost of product, shipping,cost of returned merchandise somehow damaged/used which you can no longer resell as new. Is there any money left?
												- Amazon takes 15%
												- we offer free shipping, so that take out another 9-10% from the margins.
											- The basic jist is After your product is developed you have your chinese supplier from alibaba or wherever send you pics and samples of the product. Once you're happy, you have the total order/shipment sent straight to one of amazons fba warehouses in the US. Give the first 20-100 units away, depending on what it is your selling, rack up some 5 star reviews, after about 10-20 reviews you hit the first page of amazons search for that product and hopefully the orders will start coming in. You never physically hold the product.
											- what I've gathered so far is that you'd have to invest in inventory (500-1000 units being the very minimum order quantity). Then, once you've received your order, you'd have to give away the first 100-200 units in order to get positive feedback!
												- Yeah I've heard both, selling the first 1-200 units at heavily discounted rates and giving them away.
												- 100-200 unit gIveaway is unnecessarily huge unless you are trying to be the first guy in a hyper competitive niche. Like diet supplements. In that case you have bigger worries than giving away 100-200 units as you will be selling hundreds of units per day. For this I recommend viral-launch.com and their $150 per month plan. Zonblast etc do not come close. Give away maybe 20-50 with a $1-5 price and probably 50% will leave reviews. 10-20 reviews is certainly enough to get the ball rolling.
												- A good Facebook group is amazon fba private label sellers. My mastermind group has also been invaluable. I can't give it out publicly but poke around and the interwebs and you'll find people
												- many guys do take delivery for their imported stuff, especially for the first order to do a quality check.
													- Personally I don't want to fuck with it and I don't have an address so I take delivery of the sample and if it is good I ship directly from China to the amazon fba warehouse, which is so insanely easy I couldn't believe it
											- One guys experience
											  source:: https://www.rooshvforum.com/thread-47765-post-1037898.html#pid1037898
												- $4K Amazon Amazing Selling machine course summary
													- Gist was buy an inexpensive product in bulk preferably consumables or stuff easily lost and reordered
														- fishing tackle and lures or trout, bass or saltwater flies, or ladies garments - yoga pants, workout apparel - lightweight high markup stuff that is easy to ship ...
													- then inspect for quality as you package the items in your own branded packaging then sell your brand for about half of the premium "licensed" stuff.
												- Back to Amazon once you private label your quality controlled products (Don't want lots of returns and charge-backs to Amazon as with their 15% fees you can lose money at double your cost on just one crap quality shipment). People bend over backwards to keep customers happy so they will not be dropped by Amazon - cheap Chinese Phones or buggy electronics will drive customers into rages whereas a slutty pair of panties a sloe loses in the backseat of her boyfriends mud mobile means nothing... Fisherman lose millions of lures and flies and gear a year on snags in the countries lakes and streams it is a cost of fishing.
												- Guns and ammo and crossbows are often governed at the state and Federal ATM (Fed Gun Dealers License) and huge liability if a customer shoots his girlfriend if he finds her in bed with her boss or the local bad boy wannabee -
												- Any thing seen as healthy or green, light weight and consumable or on the other hand sexy and slutty and easy to ship with large margins will sell.
												- Dried fruit, nuts and spices can be lucrative but perishables - fresh cooked gourmet meals, etc., require special handling not well suited to Amazon FBA.
												- There are manufacturers who make stuff in volume, brokers who broker by the container full and dealers who buy wholesale then private label brand and resale with merchantability and fitness implied warranties
												- Still have to stay within the bounds of the Amazon TOU.
												- So that is the short overview about FBA with Amazon - I do not like drop shipping as there is too much room for fraud and if you get a low quality shipment from a bait and switch Chinese supplier you can get in legal hot water especially if any life safety issues - flammable fabrics etc.
												- Again quite a good bit of IM discussions on the warrior forum... yeah with the name warrior forum you might think it is a military oriented forum - nope just wannabee internet marketing warriors - a few the real deal.
												- Warning once someone finds a hot niche with a few hot products they guard that info and their sources with their lives. So unfortunately a certain amount of trial and error involved.
											- Another experience
											  source:: http://www.nichepursuits.com/amazon-fba-in-30-days/
											- Pro tip: keep the product in the impulse but range...$20-50. Always use x9.95 or x9.99 (look up pricing psychology if you don't believe me...it's straight up business red pill). I prefer a price of $35.95 to qualify for free shipping. On a $5 product this is a nice margin that gives room for marketing and assorted bullshit.
										- ((66a2d2e3-8ead-45bf-9d6c-cb50ffaadfca))
										- Dropshipping
										  collapsed:: true
											- Pros
											- Cons
												- Smaller margins than having inventory
												- Unbranded so not building a brand
												- Competing with many people due to low entry
													- Unless you invest lots of time to find the best suppliers
												- Difficult to find dropship suppliers
												- Unethical dropship suppliers may steal customer info to send them catalogs
												- Can't be combined with Amazon or Ebay
													- I'd be careful of drop shipping. Ebay is doing a big purge right now asking for invoices from suppliers, if you dropship you don't have invoices and will probably get a ban unless you are good with photoshop and they don't call to verify.
											- Comparison vs keeping stock
											  id:: 66c613d2-ce8c-4665-ab67-86efd123d8be
											  collapsed:: true
												- 1
													- for example for MTG there are "command zone trays" which people make and can be used to track commander damage, commander tax, life totals etc. whilst you could just order from their ecommerce site whenever you get an order and instead put in your customer's address, there's a few limitations:
														- The manufacturer will get your full customer list info in the process, so they could potentially cut you out of the transaction as middleman by marketing to them directly
														- You won't be able to control what goes into the package - the manufacturer might place additional marketing materials of their own, you can't include marketing materials of your own, there might be an included invoice that reveals how much you're marking up the product, there might be quality assurance issues, you have limited visibility and control over shipping incase a customer has issues, etc
														- It'd be obvious you're dropshipping, so this will affect your ability to negotiate for more lenient discounts, shipping options etc
														- Buying in bulk allows you to negotiate better discounts
														  to be fair a lot of this is only relevant for established businesses - if you're instead just at the stage of testing with a minimum viable product then it's less optimal but they can be safely ignored, good to be aware of these pitfalls though
												- [07:39, 13/08/2024] Aaron: In lots of niches you'll find specialty products on random ecommerce sites, a MVP might be just buying in bulk from there whilst negotiating a discount, and only later when it proves viable instead negotiate ordering directly for a steeper discount.
												- Getting involved in the manufacturing process yourself is more advanced but very viable, you need a strong idea of what you want made though
												  [07:42, 13/08/2024] Aaron: Another one I've seen is doing modifications to a product before reselling eg flashing phones with custom Android ROMs, making a NAS out of a Raspberry Pi etc. Not viable for DS as you need your hands on the stock
												  [07:44, 13/08/2024] Aaron: This requires a bit of effort and start-up capital but there's a whole lot less competition usually which makes it worth it
												- doesn't even have to be expert tech tbf, there's people who make prebuilt PCs out of individual parts, who carve wood into sculptures, paint warhammer 40k figurines for others, etc there's lots of ways to value-add

										  collapsed:: true
											- Things You Can Sell For Example:
												- The opportunities are limitless, you can sell whatever you want to and are either limited only by the law of where you are and where your customers are or by how much you can afford to invest/work/find products.
													- Pharmaceuticals and Supplements
													- Replica Shoes, Bags, Clothes
													- Electronics (Replica or Original)
													- Specialty Products
													- Womens Jewelry and Perfumes
													- Services
													- and more
												- But BQ, how do we know what to sell?
												- Without going too much into it, it's all in the research. Thankfully with the rapidly declining privacy allotted to most people, more data is available than ever before.
												- Do your research through keyword research tools
												- The research goes like this.
											- Look for trending topics, products, and things that are selling well
											- Figure out your target demographic and isolate a problem within that group.
											- Find out where that group hangs out online, whether it is facebook, twitter, theonion, wherever. Find out how much it would cost to advertise there.
											  collapsed:: true
												- -Source, create, or even make a fake product and design a few landing pages and sales funnel.
												- -Test traffic from that source to your product, if it converts well, great you are lucky, pat yourself on the back. If it does OK, that's still good that means there's interest you just need to fix your page or some part of your campaign.
												- -Get the real product and sales funnel done, buy more traffic, optimize by changing elements of the sales page, try different email copies, etc. etc and see what works the best. Test different banners.
												- -After you are getting traffic, don't just go for the kill, establish a RELATIONSHIP with your customer, a client that comes back for more of your products is worth more than one person who comes one time as them coming back regularly means recurring income.
												- -Diversify your traffic sources, find other sites or ways to get your target demographic to come to your product, partner with others that have similiar traffic and do exchanges or even list blasts. The possibilities are endless.
												- -Flip your site or hold onto it and make more.
												- It's not that easy, but this is a long datasheet and I just felt like going into that as much as I can as quickly as I can.
												- Places You Can Sell Your Stock:
												- 1. Direct through websites:
												- Without spoonfeeding too much, think craigslist, ebay, facebook, (psst Jackthreads), or any other smaller site (these are the goldmines, search for websites specifically relevant to your niche as these tend not to be too oversaturated if you provide a quality product)
												- If on the otherhand it's information or a digital product:
												- Forums, Site Owners with lists, if it's a website Flippa, amazon, and more.
												- 2. On your own website
												- This, is where I have seen some Ridiculous money come in especially. The traffic sources above that funnel into your own website whether E-commerce or otherwise can especially do some pretty outstanding things.
												- For your own website you will need:
												- -A solid e-commerce script
												- -a trustworthy offshore host (don't trust the bullshit ones, do your due diligence and research) Extrawinner from Blackhatworld had an outstanding service, but when I come back through if recs are asked for I'll post two of my favorites or PM you (preferred).
												- -offshore domain service OR register your domain with fake details, you basically use a VCC (virtual credit card) that is created by someone that is preloaded with an amount you ask for. They are typically used to verify paypals with incorrect information to stay anonymous.
												- -an offshore VPS + cloud proxy + encryption software, again, I will have a tiny datasheet addon of recs I can pm you if you like, this is moreso to give you an idea so you can do as you like.
												- -Content + Form Capture Software such as Wufoo or mailchimp.
												- Places You Can Source What You Seek:
												- If you source online it will be a bit more expensive there are dropshipper lists that go around of quality middlemen that provide electronics, clothing etc. etc. that you can flip for a solid profit margin.
												- BUT!
												- Sourcing in other countries, such as getting software made in China/EE/India is much better for starting a legit business. If you are going the replica route for instance, let's say for example Coach Bags, sourcing in person in another country is better. For products that are replicas that people will scrutinze heavily such as that, having the best quality product is a neccesity and you will need to assure you are getting the best quality possible. A+++ quality bags (as every replica wholesaler likes to say) is hard to find that will pass the test. I have heard of ioffer.com having some of the best replicas for clothing PERIOD back in the older days but now they are more of a premium price which still offers large margins.
												- For instance, in China, Francis Tse used to be the standard for being the best large wholesaler of replica items, it became a brand in itself amongst replica wholesalers, now, everyone claims it, but if you can find legit Francis Tse connections, you can make REAL money. But, as I said before be aware of the possible consequences. LV has a f**king task force/private investigator team.

											- Nowadays I am basically a retailer for health & beauty products. I source them wholesale and resell them at a markup. It's 90% automated, quite similar to Ferriss' thing in his book.
										- Cedonulli overview
										  collapsed:: true
											- you want to sell a product online. you don’t know what to sell, and you don’t have the location or money to have inventory and ship. basically, you are starting from scratch.
											- so you find a product on amazon or ebay that sells really cheap, and is poorly marketed. say you find a dollar widget, that has potential. you take this dollar widget, make it look nice, and put it up for sale for ten bucks on another site. if you found the widget on ebay, you’ll sell it on amazon, and vice versa. with this, you start moving into some capital, build a customer marketing list, and figure out which products sell.
											- likewise, you might get into drop shipping, same sort of premise. no need to build inventory or ship, minimal capital to experiment to find out what sort of widget you can sell. once you know what sells, and you have a target demographic, and a trusting customer base, and most importantly some capital, you move into the next phase.
											- you start sourcing the widget yourself. instead of paying a buck for the widget, you might now pay 10 cents. you use third party fulfilment to ship the widget, and you now also have the funds to brand the widget to branch out into related widgets to your customer base.
											- on the flip side: i never made fuck-all, drop shipping. for all it’s advantages, you’re also totally exposed and competing against everybody else who ever caught on to the basic principle of arbitrage-via-internets. you’re still busting your ass, unless or until you get smarter than all the other guys. as starting somewhere goes though, it’s definitely somewhere.

										- 6 Foundational Ecommerce Marketing Tactics to Stop Your Bucket from Leaking – Shopify
										  collapsed:: true
											- So What Exactly Is DODOcase Doing Right?
											  collapsed:: true
												- As you can see from the short case study above, they're doing a lot of things right and have a pretty comprehensive marketing plan in place to make the most of all their traffic, paid and organic. They have implemented several key tactics to keep people in their bucket, to bring qualified visitors back, and to increase the average order size from customers.
												- Let's take a look at each one in a little more detail:
												- Product Upsell - Upsells are important because they give you one last opportunity to present the customer with complimentary products that a.) they may be interested in, b.) might make their experience better (think batteries if they purchased something that doesn’t come with batteries) and c.) help increase your average order value. If that's not enough, one analyst for Forrester said that product recommendations (upsells) are responsible for an average of 10-30% of ecommerce site revenues.
												- Newsletter Sign Up Popup - Upon trying to leave the site (exit intent), realizing you don’t want to purchase or aren’t ready to purchase, they display a last second, secondary call-to-action. In this case they try to get you to sign up to their newsletter while also providing you with 10% off in the hope that the 10% will sway you over to completing a purchase with them. Although it's important to note that popup signups to some people are considered annoying, in almost every study they convert higher, many times 100%+ better than standard signup forms embedded on your site. The emails collected from a popup can be instrumental in bringing back visitors and don't forget, emails is consistently rated as the best marketing channel for return-on-investment.
												- Abandoned Cart Email - Abandoned cart emails are vital in ecommerce. An average of 68% of people abandon their carts. These are people that had enough interest to browse your products and add items to their cart. They completed the first step of the checkout but decided to abandon their carts at that point. Abandoned cart emails give you an opportunity to bring that customer back to complete their transaction while it's still fresh in their minds.
												- Adwords Retargeting - The next day DODOcase began retargeting with display ads on the Google Display Ads network. Retargeting helps reinforce your brand and serve as a reminder to your recent site visitors of your products. In this particular case, DODOcase offered 20% to come back. Retargeting ads have proven to be much more effective than traditional online ads. In fact, in one study web site visitors who were retargeted with display ads were 70% more likely to convert.
												- Facebook Retargeting - Again, several days later, they began retargeting on Facebook with the same 20% off deal. Combined with the Adwords retargeting, and they were unavoidable if you had an internet connection.
												- Referral Offer - Upon completing an order, DODOcase offers their customer $20 credit for referring friends in addition to giving all referrals 15% off their first purchase. Referral marketing is incredible important as it represents the perfect opportunity to get your most recent and happy customers to talk about you. Study after study has come to the same conclusion, that people trust and purchase products more from a friend recommendation than any other form of marketing. In fact, according to Neilsen, people are four times more likely to purchase a product when referred by a friend.
											- How Can You Plug the Holes?
											  collapsed:: true
												- The six marketing tactics discussed above make for a rock solid foundation for your ecommerce business and help you plug some of the biggest holes, leaving you and your site, much more prepared for paid and organic traffic alike.
												- To begin implementing these tactics yourself, we have rounded up some incredible Shopify Apps to help you get started right away:
												- Upsell - Shopify App: Product Upsell
												- Email Popup - Shopify App: Pop-Up Window
												- Abandon Cart Recovery - Built into Shopify or Shopify App:Klaviyo
												- Google Retargeting - Shopify App - Adroll
												- Facebook Retargeting - Shopify App - PropelAd
												- Referrals - Shopify App - Forewards

									- [quote='redbeard' pid='1673254' dateline='1509253464']
									- Excellent data sheet.
									- I want to add on to this: [b]The most difficult part is writing.[/b]
									- [quote='This Is Trouble' pid='1671671' dateline='1509014093']
									- [b]"I don't like writing. What do I do?"[/b]
									- The most difficult part is writing. You should try to sit down and do a little bit each day. Try to tell stories about your personal life and then tie them into the theme of your site, or whatever it is that you are selling. They are far, far easier to write than trying just to pound out generic product reviews or speculation.
									- Read [url=http://bensettle.com/]Ben Settle[/url] if you need some tips on how to do this properly.
									- Again, you must pick something that, starting out, you can at least tolerate. Don't try to pick a subject you know nothing about or have no interest in. At the same time, don't copy everything I've done exactly. Only I can be Kyle. You have to be you, or else it just won't work. I know that sounds incredibly cliche, but it's also incredibly true. Find your own voice, or...
									- [/quote]
									- It doesn't matter who you are or which niche you want to get into, having good writing skills will take your business from A to B faster then anything else.
									- I've been at this internet thing for a while but it didn't start clicking until a few months ago, when I started writing [i]constantly[/i]. I did engineering in college so of course, writing is not my strong suit, but I was determined to improve. Every good writer I spoke with suggested the same remedy to bad writing: more writing (thanks WIA).
									- So I started writing a fuck ton, for practice. Hell the other night I zoned out and wrote 1800 words about NoFap.
									- Anything you write counts as practice. This forum post counts. Longform Facebook posts, FB group value posts, and even emails count.
									- Now, I use emails and Facebook as my writing practice AND lead maturation tactics.
									- Having an email list is crucial, especially if you're YOU'RE the face of the brand. You have to keep them warmed up and interested in what you have to offer.
									- Once you have a list, email them constantly. "Gurus" will argue over how much, but 3 times a week is a good starting point. Ben Settle (who I love) emails [i]at least[/i] once a day. Remember this writing is also sharpening your writing blade, so do it often.
									- "Uh redbeard, what am I going to write about everyday?"
									- When you start out, just write about something. Even better, write about nothing. Focus on quantity over quality.
									- It's just like approaching girls. A shitty approach is better then no approach. As long as you write, you're improving your skills and keeping in touch with your list.
									- A tactic this forum will like is Russell Brunson's "Daily Seinfeld Emails" script. In DotComSecrets, he talks about how the introduction to Seinfeld inspires his email content. You guys know what I'm talking about...
									- "What's the deal with airport security?"
									- The technique is to treat your emails like something Seinfeld would talk about. Just banter about what's going on in your life, but somehow tie it back into the topic of the blog.
									- For example, with OP's Ukraine blog, he could talk about this interesting coffee place he went to, or something that got lost in translation from Ukrainian to English.
									- Here's an illustration from the book:
									- [img]http://www.dominickpirone.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/11/daily-seinfeld-emails.jpg[/img]
									- All you have to do is pick a topic and run with it.
									- Something I've been doing for a Facebook group I run is to do this every morning:
										- 1. Read a book
										- 2. Decide my biggest lesson from today's reading
										- 3. Take that lesson, put my spin on it, and write 300-400 words about it to post in the group.
									- It provides great value to the group, writing practice to me, and keeps me accountable for reading.
									- You could do the same thing with any niche or authority site. For example on a pickup blog, you could write about yesterday's approaches, or something going on with your relationships, Carrie Bradshaw style.
									- If you're a shitty writer, you have all the tools in front of you to improve.
									- The rest is up to you...
									- [/quote]
								- Market / Product
									- Market and Product
									- There aren’t too many aspects to worry about here:
									- To identify a lucrative market, you have to see what folks want to buy, find something similar and offer it to them. Or you can be a stubborn fool and try to get them to buy some crap they don’t want. Stay broke, I don’t care. This is one of the most important decisions of the process and you should make it consciously, even if you think you don’t need to.
									- If you can be passionate about your market and product, all the better. But Don’t-Give-A-Crap marketing (when disciplined) will always outsell Fool-In-Love-With-His-Dream marketing. No exceptions. If you can’t have both, take the first. Make some money. Then you can at least become a sucker and pay for it.
									- People pay the most when you solve a problem. So search for phrases on Google like “no solution” and “need help” and so forth. Forums are good, so add the word “forum.” See what comes up. Find the ones that come up most often. Do some reading. Then see if you can find or make a product that solves their problem. This will define your niche and product at the same time. (Niche means small specialized market.)
									- Also, you could look into desperate people (people with eminent money disasters, especially home foreclosures, spouses with fear of being cheated on, insecure people who cannot get dates, those with severe diseases, fat people, etc.). This is really predatory if you do not provide value, but if you do and you over-deliver, you will make good money and provide them a service they sorely want and need. Not everyone is suited to this category, though, and you could end up a nervous wreck who is unable to sleep at nights from guilt and empathy. Check whether you have more of an inner moral compass or a callous where that should be.
									- The best stuff is digital because there’s no overhead, but you know what you know. So if your product is physical and there is shipping, deal with it.
									- Then look on Amazon, eBay and other places that provide product sales statistics. See how the products sell for this problem and niche. There are all kinds of techniques for checking this, so look for them in the literature. The only important part is to be sure you are looking atclosed sales statistics and not simply product searches or offers.
									- On keywords, you’ve got to do the donkeywork. Keep in mind that when a person types in a keyword on Google or a search engine, he is looking for something. You need to get him to look at your stuff and not someone else’s stuff. Do what it takes. It’s easy to lose sight of this when you start studying it because of all the math. There are a gazillion books on keyword strategies, but you can actually get the basics for free by searching Google.
									- Product names make the best keywords. Next come 3-5 word phrases called long-tailed phrases. The worst keywords are general terms like “make money” and the worst of the worst are ones nobody is looking for. You type them in and nothing comes up except
									- “sorry.”
									- Once again, make sure people who search for your keywords are people who buy stuff (do Amazon, eBay, etc., again if you need to). “Freeware,” for instance, is a terrible keyword. So are “cheapskate” and “free ebooks.”
									- The highest-selling niche markets are those whose keywords have:
										- 1. A lot of search requests (over 200 a month on the free Wordtracker keyword tool), and
										- 2. A low number of pages to search (under 300,000 or so on a Google search).
									- This is just a rule of thumb, though. If everybody is selling Wii’s and making money, then sell Wii’s, too. When everybody’s doing it, unless you want to specialize, don’t compete. Copy. Copy everything you can from top sellers and expose yourself as much as you can to their traffic. Expect modest earnings, not a fortune from this.
									- You can sell all kinds of stuff, both niche and popular. If you sell enough modest stuff, you make a fortune.
									- Next you need a site to sell the stuff on. Learn HTML sites, do Wordpress, or set up a free site like a hosted blog, Squidoo or a Web 2.0 site. Learn the options. There’s tons of assorted crap out there to teach you. For a small niche site, you need about 5-10 pages of quality content and your offer, which will send the reader to a sales page. Make it pretty to look at and that’s about it. You can pepper it with Adsense if sales are down.
									- Don’t be fooled by a lot of blah blah blah about blogging. Treat blogs as niche presales sites or backlink pages. End of blogging course.
									- You can make Adsense sites with blog farms, etc., but products give you more money per sale. Learn to sell products. Then learn to make Adsense sites. That’s the best sequence in my opinion unless you’re a real geek or math freak.
									- If you can learn the shit-through-the-snake sales page techniques, all the better. (This means your reader starts at the top, goes through a long scroll until he hits bottom and comes out by clicking to the order page.) Lots of hype, testimonials, fake high prices that you discount, fake reviews, freebies, big ugly “Buy Now” buttons all over, and other techniques. There’s oodles of stuff on this. They are usually called landing pages or sales pages. They are crap but they work. That’s why people use them.
									- If you have trouble learning the techniques, find a good sales page that impresses you and copy the damn thing. Then replace the dude’s stuff with your info. Sales pages don’t get any search engine attention, so you can copy up a storm.
									- IMPORTANT. Make sure you can get paid and accept credit card payments. Set up accounts with Paypal, Clickbank, some other places (look for them). Get a merchant account (the best option, but do the others as well).
									- Make sure you deliver promptly. For digital products, make sure there is a way to download it. Those who sell other people’s stuff online don’t have that problem, but they need to learn how to cloak their commission link (usually called a hoplink). It’s pretty technical but doable. Even if you don’t do it, you still make money. You are just ripped off sometimes.
									- If you are starting out with your own digital product, protect the download page, but not too rigorously. It’s good for Black Hatters and other clever fellows and gals to grab it for free and comment about it. Your name grows as it spreads. They’re going to steal it anyway, so don’t even worry about it. Just make it a little challenging to keep the newbies out. Of course, it’s better to get paid, but remember that if you completely isolate yourself and you are a nobody, you will get 100% of nothing.
									- As to physical products, like I said, there’s shipping and you have to deal with it. This isn’t rocket science, though and the literature is easy to find. Look for “drop shipping” and you will get all kinds of places to sell goods and let others worry about shipping.
									- I didn’t say too much about one aspect of your product: what you sell. You can sell other people’s stuff, your own stuff or try to get them to do stuff like click on an ad, fill out forms or install programs on their computers. I didn’t say much on purpose. There are gazillions of ideas, techniques and so-called secrets out there. Find what tickles your fancy, find the instructions for getting it or making it on a Google search and simply do that.
									- But none of this is nearly as important as making sure your product or information solves the problem of people who buy stuff. Or it tickles their dream or they think it’s cool. If you get to them and offer them they want, you sell. That’s the only real secret and any kindergarten kid can tell you that.
									- Make sure you choose decent profits, too. At least $15-20 a sale. Otherwise, don’t waste your time.
									- On advertising, I will deal with this below.
									- White Hatters fall in love with their product and market regardless of sales. Gray Hatters do the market research donkeywork and sell what people want. Black Hatters sell home loans and penis enlargers and so forth by spam with no research, or try to get people to do stuff often by tricking them.
									- As regards customer profile, remember that it is easier to sell one person out of 2,000 an item (and a dream) for $2,000 than it is to get 2,000 people to buy something for a dollar. You only need to lay down some bullshit to get the first, but you need a whole lot of bullshit to get the second and the money is the same. There is a lot of literature on this so make sure you become familiar with it. I will go into it a bit below on BS Riches Tributary.
								- _Related: _Product Creation
								  #ProductCreation https://workflowy.com/#/c2c4024dcfa0
						- Franchise model
						  collapsed:: true
							- I learned a long time ago that having staff was not for me. Too many headaches.
							- As a result of this I structured my business from the off as a franchise model that fits around my life. A few ball busting years at the beginning have given way to something that requires very little input from me beyond motivation and training others to do the same.
							- Too many people start businesses with no idea what they actually want out of it and therefore do not structure accordingly.

The obligations of confidentiality will apply during the term of this agreement and will survive indefinitely upon termination of this agreement.
											- All written and oral information and material disclosed or provided by the client to the contractor under this agreement is confidential information regardless of whether it was provided before or after the date of this agreement or how it was provided to the contractor.

											- All other intellectual property and related material that is developed or produced under this agreement will be the sole property of the contractor. The use of intellectual property by the contractor will not be restricted in any manner. The client will retain access to all intellectual property and related material that is required for continued operation of their business.
