				- Service Agreement
				- Systems are documented to allow an easy transition..
				- No marriage allowed during contract - you must offer to leave before a business
				- Plan for Success
					- Lifestyle Goals
					- Money Goals
					- e.g. look into timeframes, willingness to invest, what you expect, any big purchases you want to make in the near future e.g. houses
				- Marriage Model of Business Partnerships/Exclusivity Deal
				  Don't like the sound of it as first impression
					- Pros/Cons
						- Pros
							- More skin in the game - more invested to make things work
							- It allows the partner to not have resentment about any side projects you work on because you know they're getting brought in on it
						- Cons
							- Partner may not have the resources to be a good partner on all business/investment types
					- All side projects are shared e.g. if one person starts a surf shop then it's automatically shared
					- All assets are now 50:50 unless we create an exception or create a deal
					- e.g. creating blogs on the side means you're not focusin on the core business
					- You get a 50% stake regardless
				- Write down on a word document what time commitment and tasks you'd each commit to in order to prevent any unnecessary arguments
					- Until someone can commit say 10 hours a week for 3 months minimum it's kind of hard to get anything off the group. Solo you can be flexible, but as a partnership you'd want more fixed progression
				- Managing resentment
					- They need to contribute every year
					- Make a plan for success
						- Exit plans
						- Ongoing su
						- What ongoing roles
					- Don't partner for cash - instead get loans, etc because otherwise you lose too much equity
					- Don't partner if you're very unequal with financial standing - otherwise you value different things
					- Don't partner and give up time - instead cash/staff/infrastructure. Because your desires change over time
					- Avoid the "race to do less"
						- Make sure
						- Memorandum of Understanding
							- List all partners
							- List resources to be added
								- Specific
								- Avoid time commitments e.g. 5 hours
								- Instead focus on deliverables, resources, results
							- Revenue benchmarks and what should be discussed at each benchmark
							- Milestones
					- Have a strong divide between what tasks each are in charge of
					- Create timelines
						- When to call it quits
						- Expected results
					- Accept compromises
						- Unequal time commitments/division of labour
						- Financial investment
						- Financial risk appetite
						- Skillsets and experiences
						- Connections
						- Industry knowledge and expertise
					- Avoid "who can do less and still get paid the same"
					- Concrete division of labour ideally - less conflict because people have different ideas for how to do things
				- Regularly Scheduled Communication
					- Conference calls
					- Make sure to bring up any potential issues early
				- Put Everything in Writing with a Partnership Agreement
					- A partnership agreement is the business version of a prenuptial agreement and should be completed before you start operations and any profits are made (the division of profits is a critical part of this process). Although an agreement is not legally required, it can protect your interests as one half of the partnership for the duration of your partnership and through its dissolution.
					- Things to include in the agreement include the following:
					- Division of profits – This includes both the division of profits and losses and how and when each partner will get paid.
					- Contributions to the partnership – If either partner contributes any assets to the business, whether it’s cash, property, or equipment, you’ll need to ensure these are documented.
					- Business decision-making – What authority does each partner have to make business decisions? How will you handle disputes? How will you handle the dissolution of the partnership when that time comes?
					- Who does what – Divide up your management duties and document them here. For example, who handles media relations, payroll, etc.
					- Work with a lawyer and your accountant to develop and formalize the agreement, there are many factors that require consideration when forming any kind of partnership and getting legal and financial advice now will save you a lot of hassle in the long run.
				- Be Specific When Defining Roles and Responsibilities, But Allow for Cross-Training
					- While initially delineating roles and responsibilities is essential, it is probably not enough. As businesses grow and evolve, so do the roles of the partners. It is one thing to decide initially that partner A has her set of tasks, while partner B has his, but what happens when new tasks surface? An ongoing conversation is necessary to keep the roles well-defined.
					- Also, circling back periodically to redefine responsibilities has the added benefit of highlighting potential areas of cross-training. Businesses that are run with "islands" of responsibility are much more likely to suffer from devastating results from small setbacks, like a short-term illness of one of the partners, than businesses than that have multiple avenues to complete essential tasks.
				- Possible problems
				- How to call it quits
					- Buy the other person out
				- **Sections**
					- Individual Contribution section
						- Creative CV for Hourly Hotel App
							- Role - Head of Growth
							- Main expertise areas:
								- Marketing Strategy
								- Business Development
									- Startup Stage
										- Standard Operating Procedures for marketing, customer service, DevOps for website development, systems administration, infrastructure
									- Management
										- Project management, employee/freelancer management
							- Experience
								- Marketing
									- Responsible for bringing in 6 figures of revenue across several internet-based businesses
							- Expectations
								-
								- Expectations
									- Compensation
										- Base salary: £500 monthly
										- Performance-based commission based on monthly net profit
											- Commission levels
												- £1-10k = 10%
												- £10k+ = 20% cap
											- _For comparison_
												- A close friend recently took up a similar offer in the exact same role for a biz already making $10k/month net profit:
													- $0-15k (£0-10k) = 15%
													- $15k+ (£10k+) = 20%
													- $20k+ (£14k+) = 25%
													- $25k+ (£17k+) = 30% cap
													- Table with figures showing split between Josh + 2 cofounders

									- Sizeable marketing budget
									- For software, advertising, freelancers, etc
										- e.g. £3k+
										- And scales up with increasing revenue
								- Goals, Preferences and Limitations
									- _Overview_
										- This section is for being open about what you think your role could be at this point in time, as well as in the future
									- Communication
										- Due to my other night shift work and personal preferences I will work remotely likely most of the time
										- I am amenable to regular audio/video/in-person meetings with an agenda and 24/7 chat
										- I prefer to chat via Slack (instant messaging website + mobile app) as it integrates well with other business software + has great archival and search features

								- Expectations
									- Compensation
										- Base salary: £500 monthly
										- Performance-based commission based on monthly net profit
											- Commission levels
												- £1-10k = 10%
												- £10k+ = 20% cap
											- _For comparison_
												- A close friend recently took up a similar offer in the exact same role for a biz already making $10k/month net profit:
													- $0-15k (£0-10k) = 15%
													- $15k+ (£10k+) = 20%
													- $20k+ (£14k+) = 25%
													- $25k+ (£17k+) = 30% cap
													- Table with figures showing split between Josh + 2 cofounders
													- http://imgur.com/CGLa6fa
									- Sizeable marketing budget
									- For software, advertising, freelancers, etc
										- e.g. £3k+
										- And scales up with increasing revenue
								- Other Info
									- Communication
										- Due to my other night shift work and personal preferences I will work remotely likely most of the time
										- I am amenable to regular audio/video/in-person meetings with an agenda and 24/7 chat
										- I prefer to chat via Slack (instant messaging website + mobile app) as it integrates well with other business software + has great archival and search features
					- Equity & Shares
						- There's some mix of work, money, know-how, experience, equipment already owned and, maybe, the idea.
						- Later joiners will gain more shares which will dilute ownership
							- Only shares distributed to initial Core Founders have voting rights
							- They get less ownership because they took less risk
						- Shares distributed later will be non-voting
						  Ordinary Shares Class B”
						- Vesting Schedule
							- _Overview_
								- Equity is not given, it is earned. Thus put together a list of what you think you can offer the business and these can be used to benchmark whether you're working to your own expectations
							- This tends to solve many problems for startups especially when:
								- You can’t pay an attractive salary.
								- You want to fire someone if they’re not suited.
							- No shares will be vested unless you've been working on the business for a year
								- Or possibly 0.000001% of ownership immediately given to each person
							- If leaving the company you must offer  required to offer them for sale to the company or to an existing Core Founder shareholder (at fair value).
							- Their family situation might change, they might get an amazing job offer, or wish to move to another country.
							- Now that we have a fair system set out, there is one important principle. You must have vesting. Preferably 4 or 5 years. Nobody earns their shares until they've stayed with the company for a year. A good vesting schedule is 25% in the first year, 2% each additional month. Otherwise your co-founder is going to quit after three weeks and show up, 7 years later, claiming he owns 25% of the company. It never makes sense to give anyone equity without vesting.
							- This is an extremely common mistake and it's terrible when it happens. You have these companies where 3 co-founders have been working day and night for five years, and then you discover there's some jerk that quit after two weeks and he still thinks he owns 25% of the company for his two weeks of work.
							- In the case of shares in a company (the most common form for a business in the UK) it means that the shares are yours. The ‘schedule’ part of ‘vesting schedule’ refers to what is usually a table that sets out how many shares will vest and when. There are two kinds of vesting: ordinary (where the shares are issued to you as you go along) or reverse (where a specific share allocation is provided for you but vests as you go along).
							- Now ‘go along’ can take a number of forms and might include: Time, meaning the time spent working in the company and this can be in days, weeks, months or whatever time intervals are chosen. In the US the norm is for a four-year vesting period with a one year ‘cliff’. A cliff means that nothing vests unless and until you pass a specified date, commonly a year from commencement.
							- Performance: shares vest on achieving certain milestones or targets, whether individually or as a company. In many situations a start-up does not need to establish a company at the outset (after all, doing so means there will be audit costs to pay and Companies House compliance issues such as appointment of directors, annual returns, etc). Where a company has not been formed what may be needed is a collaboration agreement, an example of which is made freely available by Seedcamp which recently published the second edition of their draft agreement.
							- The Seedcamp agreement makes no provision as to what happens if a start-up company is not formed, particularly with regard to IP created in the pre-company formation stage. A suitable non-disclosure agreement can provide a base level of recognition and protection for what individuals bring to the table however the new IP (such as in coding or design) created during the collaboration period may be of value to someone and therefore a potential source of dispute.
							- If noone wants to form a company then it’s better perhaps to let everyone involved make use of what was created and good luck to them. Whatever is preferred it should be discussed and put in writing. Having a draft agreement is not enough, nor is instructing lawyers going to help; if you have not taken the time to sit down together to have a grown up conversation about who is contributing what and what you expect of each other.
							- If you decide to instruct lawyers they have to know what you want and you can save a lot of time and money if you can thrash things out between you first and write it down (nothing like writing things down to focus minds). Deciding what value to attribute to each person’s contribution is itself a challenge and I have struggled to find resources that report how this is commonly done, save for the toll I came across by chance at www.foundrs.com.
							- Time :  You may be required to hold the shares for X years before you’re entitled to them. The shares may be granted on a staggered basis over a period of time (known as graded vesting) or all at once (known as cliff vesting). Equally the vested shares may be vested on a uniform basis (ie 25% of the shares each year for 4 years) or on a non-uniform basis (ie 20%, 20% and 60% over years 1-3);
							- Event : Your right to them arises on a certain event such as an IPO or a change of control of the company;
							- Target : Personal or corporate performance target i.e. achieving a certain level of sales.
					- Capital Strategy
						- How much to be invested, when, by whom, how often
						- Investment by Core Founders
						- Investment by non-Core Founders
							- Avoid capital investment in exchange for ownership. Instead, bootstrap the business to pay for itself by quickly creating a Minimum Viable Product
						- Profit Distribution
							- None to be made until business is generating at least £10,000/month in net profit OR business bank account retains minimum £50,000. Thus buffer is kept for reinvestment into the business. Actual figures should be revisited when revenue reaches certain milestones.
							- If someone chooses not to take a salary at this point, a ledger is kept and they are allowed to take that amount in a lump sum at a later time as long as the above criteria is still being met.
							- Funds will not be returned on a 1:1 basis to Core Founders depending on how much was invested into the project unless specifically agreed upon beforehand. This is to account for the myriad of ways that value can be invested into the project.
							- Amongst Core Founders funds will be distributed according to shareholders equity ratio related to each other. This means that at any point everyone will receive equal profit despite not all shares having vested.
					- Decision Making
						- Most decisions should be left up to the person in charge of that area of business
						- Up to £XXX these decisions can be made without prior consulting
					- Planning for Failure
					- Planning for Success
						- Right of First Refusal
							- If someone decides to sell their equity, there should first be an offer for the company to buy out that interest, and if the company refuses, an offer for the remaining equity holders to purchase a pro rata share of that interest. Only then, whatever remains, can it be sold to the intended party.
							- Example
								- Abe owns a house and Bo offers to buy that house for $1 million. However, Carl holds a right of first refusal to purchase the house. Therefore, before Abe can sell the house to Bo, he must first offer it to Carl for the $1 million that Bo is willing to buy it for. If Carl accepts, he buys the house instead of Bo. If Carl declines, Bo may now buy the house at the proposed $1 million price.
					- http://www.eulawonline.uk/10-of-the-most-important-clauses-to-put-in-your-shareholder-agreement/
					- Know each other's strengths and weaknesses
			- http://linkilaw.com/get-quotes-now/

			- _[Learning Resources]_
				- Asking the forum ?
					- I have a question for you gentlemen regarding partnerships.
					- A few nights ago I was having a random discussion with three close friends when the topic segued to discussing a problem in our local area, and a business model that could solve that problem.
					- Before I knew it 2 hours had passed, I was recording notes and making a project plan for our next actions.
					- I'm curious if anyone here has had experience with partnering up with friends on business projects and what advice you would give?
					- I'm doing quite well with another business at the moment so I'm not planning on betting everything on this. On my part I expect about a £2k investment and a few dozen hours doing project management and marketing until we have a minimum viable product to carry out some practical market tests.
					- My friends have full-time jobs so this is merely a side project for us all at this stage. Personally I feel that I'm bringing the most to the table due to my business experience and their lack thereof but I don't want to get petty arguing about ownership % and who's bringing the most to the table. Will probably just vest shares over 4 years with a 1 year cliff and setup some sort of exit clause to negate worst case scenarios.
					- The main attractions to this for me is that I'd like to diversify my income streams, the market potential is quite high and I want to help my friends leave the rat race also. I expect to hear the "never do business with friends" argument but I am currently partnered with a previous acquaintance on my current business and it's going great. With the current situation however because these guys are much closer friends
					- So I'm wondering if anyone has ideas for managing expectations with written agreements, ways to solve disputes, etc?
			- _{Archive}_

		- [Entrepreneurship is like one of those carnival games where you throw darts or something.](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=15659076)
		  collapsed:: true
			- Middle class kids can afford one throw. Most miss. A few hit the target and get a small prize. A very few hit the center bullseye and get a bigger prize. Rags to riches! The American Dream lives on.
			- Rich kids can afford many throws. If they want to, they can try over and over and over again until they hit something and feel good about themselves. Some keep going until they hit the center bullseye, then they give speeches or write blog posts about "meritocracy" and the salutary effects of hard work.
			- Poor kids aren't visiting the carnival. They're the ones working it.

			- Venture Capitalism
			  collapsed:: true
				- 1
					- Discussion on VC-backed projects
					  https://discuss.privacyguides.net/t/avoiding-the-next-skiff-criteria-to-ban-vc-backed-projects/16722/68
					- What VCs do require are one of the following three things, and we need to be completely clear on this for this discussion to even work:
					- Acquisition by a larger company
					  IPO
					  Their investment to be repaid (e.g. via a share buyout)
					- I am arguing that any company that has not yet done one of these three things must not be recommended, because we do in fact know that they must do one of those three things eventually. Once they do one of those three things, then we can decide whether they should be recommended. It’s a matter of waiting.

			- Books
			  id:: 644c09ca-119b-4521-be2a-facdf22309b8
			  collapsed:: true
				- 1. "Lean Startup" (pretty standard)
				- 2. "Spin Selling" (sales)
				- 3. "The Four-Hour Work Week" (not because of the way of life promised in the misleading title, but because of the links to useful Websites, and for motivational reading)
				- 4. "The One Billion Dollar App" (silly title but fantastic book from an actual taxi app product manager - I almost didn't buy it because of the title, thank God I opened it anyway and started reading about viral marketing which like the tracking of pandemic is based on the r coefficient).
				- 5. "Business Model Generation" (the mechanics of making money)
				- 6. "The Startup Owner's Manual"
				- 7. "Why Startups Fail" (anti-patterns - better read about them before you get trapped by them)
				- 8. "The Company Secretary Handbook" (UK only)
				- 9. "Die Unternehmergesellschaft (UG): Gründung, Geschäftsführung, Recht und Steuern für kleinere Unternehmen und Start-Ups" (Germany only)
				- 10. "Founders At Work" (motivational)
				- 11. "Financial Times Essential Guides Writing a Business Plan: How to win backing to start up or grow your business" (to get clarity, write a plan - for yourself, to align all co-founders and the team, to get VC funding, to convince yourself that the business is financially viable)
				- “The Mom Test” by Rob Fitzpatrick - How to talk to customers & learn if your business is a good idea when everyone is lying to you. [Amazon.com: The Mom Test: How to talk to customers & learn if your business is a good idea when everyone is lying to you eBook : Fitzpatrick, Rob: Kindle Store](https://a.co/d/8KzUk8b)
				- "Good strategy/bad strategy" by Richard P. Rumelt
				- After the startup phase (growth/acquisition)? I recommend "The messy middle" by Scott Belsky.
				- The E-Myth - creating systems and processes
				- The Personal MBA - crash course MBA
				- Founders At Work - understanding how different startups survived
				- Buy Back Your Time - management and delegation
				- The Charisma Myth - to help w/ charm for sales
				- SPIN Selling - this + The Charisma Myth more than doubled our sales conversion rate
				- Crossing the Chasm
				- "Good to Great" by Jim Collins.
				-
			- EDP Presents: Entrepreneurship vs Intrapreneurship
			  id:: 6630c604-4b18-4a88-ab3b-f4bb15b50843
			  collapsed:: true
				- Entrepreneurial Development Programme (EDP) is a 12-week programme featuring candidates from across BAE Systems plc, delivered by the University of Oxford's Said Business School. At the end they pitch investment-ready ventures to Senior Leaders
				- David Mareels' cybersecurity software project was being shutdown, so they asked BAE if they could spin it out to their own company. BAE gave a few months timeline, and they ended up raising 2 million by angel investors and B2B. April 2022. 6-7 team. BAE retained a minority(?) stake and within 2 hours they were acquired
					- Having BAE brand behind you helps a lot with credibility if leveraged well
					- Find out what balance of being mission-driven/passion vs financial incentives
					- They had negotiation over cap terms
					- BAE had a minority stake
					- It had to look like a venture capital-backed business which is why they had a minority stake, so they looked like an early stage investor
					- It was a long process doing negotiations with BAE Legal but it got done in the end
					- The $2M pitch should be tied to specific goals and spending breakdown in X years. Not the final final end product necessarily but a significant milestone that'll allow more investment or income
					-

					- {Archive}
					  AKA new business steps
						- Initial
							- [1] Created two WorkFlowy Team Boards, added team by email to each, added a few example SOPs
								- **Team Boards**
									- [Team Dashboard]
									- [Team Infoboard]
									  Read-only
										- Standard Operating Procedures
											- Multiple Responsibility Projects SOP
												- _Overview_
													- Description
														- This SOP will teach you how to create Multiple Responsibility Projects, which are a special type of project assigned to multiple people where _everyone_ is required to do the task.
													- Benefit
														- You will be able reduce message flow in WhatsApp/Slack and ensure that no task 'falls through the gaps' (as can happen with long threads or multiple messages)
												- Definition & Examples
													- Multiple Responsibility Projects (MRPs)
														- _What are they?_
															- These are tasks that _multiple_ people have to do (not together, each must do the task)
																- If there instead is a task that only one person must do then instead just assign them the task
																- If there instead is a task that only one person is needed for but you're unsure who it is best assigned to then assign it to yourself and add the appropriate people as task followers - ask who it should be assigned to via comments
															- The MRPs may be as quick as simply noting you've read the task or it may require a response or action
														- _Why do MRPs exist?_
															- The combination of SRPs and MRPs can contain all task info that would otherwise be communicated via WhatsApp/Slack and no info is lost because any other conversations are noted within or linked from the SRP/MRP
															- It will often be used for announcements, and it's better suited as a MRP than a WhatsApp/Slack message as you can confirm to others you've read it and it hasn't been missed
												- **SOP**
													- _How to Create a MRP_
														- Open Asana - Multiple Responsibility Projects
														  https://app.asana.com/0/370692686510138/list
														- Create a new task in the top of the Project
														  http://i.imgur.com/35DpiNx.png
														- Add all relevant info - e.g. link to Slack messages, uploaded files, etc
														- Others will get an automatic notification that this task has been created, you don't need to add them as followers (unless you want additional comment discussion)
														- After creation if you don't need to do anything more on the MRP yourself then Like the task
														  e.g. if you're just making an announcement
														- This allows you to see at a glance whether there's anything you haven't reviewed
														  http://i.imgur.com/33ZloN8.png
													- _How to Complete_
														- If all you need to do is mark that you've read the MRP then first Like the task
														  http://i.imgur.com/wWPjcS6.png
														- Then click-and-drag the task to one of the 'Waiting on' sections if there's only one person left. This should be the person who hasn't yet Liked the task
														  http://i.imgur.com/1w39Pzw.png
														-
														- If there's any tasks there not for you then Like the task as well.
															- This allows you to see at a glance that there's no MRP that's waiting on you because you've Liked everything
															  http://i.imgur.com/33ZloN8.png
														- If the task now has everyone Liked it then everyone on our team has completed the MRP. You can now mark the task as complete.
												- **Note: **
													- Majority of MRPs should be in Asana - Multiple Responsibility Projects
											- _Soft Skills_
												- How to Communicate with Anyone
													- _Overview_
														- Description
															- This will teach you how effectively communicate with anyone using any medium.
														- Benefit
															- You will be able to more effectively inform, persuade and connect with others.
													- **SOP**
														- With anyone:
															- Brevity + Clarity
																- _Overview_
																	- Brevity - Minimising the amount of words used by using concise, exact and descriptive words.
																	- Clarity - Simple and easy to understand.
																- Brevity
																	- Keep messages as short as possible without being rude. Just explain the ESSENTIAL information
																		- Do not explain the whole backstory or chain of events that led to this communication.
																		- EXAMPLES
																			- "Hello there, we have tried to get it locally; however Indonesia didn't manufacture it and I got your contact information from your colleague"
																				- ^ All of this is unnecessary. It makes you sound lost, confused and not in control. Not useful when trying to persuade others
																	- After writing the whole email, ask of every sentence - If the recipient did not know this information, would it affect their ability to answer?
																	- Get to the point - what is the Next Action?
																		- People don't want to hear excessive long stories or explanations.
																		- Get to the point and make it unmistakeably clear that THIS thing right here is what you want them to do.
																		- What is the Next Action required?
																		- Use formatting, subheadlines and other devices to make the Next Action required clear too.
																- Clarity
																	- Formatting e.g. bold typeface, subheadlines, separate sections, bullet points and numbering
																	- All paragraphs should be limited 3 sentences max
																	- If a sentence looks long, split the compound sentence into two separate sentences
																	- Remove all words which add nothing
																	- Always use the most simple and clear words possible that even a 10-year old could understand
															- Give them a Benefit
																- Most people are too selfish – they focus way too much on what they want and too little on what other people want.
																	- People don't care what you want, they want to know _what's in it for them_.
																- Why giving others a benefit is **always** beneficial to you
																	- The Law of Reciprocity
																		- People generally feel obliged to return favours offered to them. Studies have shown that even if the gift is unwanted, it will influence the recipient to reciprocate.
																	- The Value Cycle
																		- Give someone value (e.g. a solution to their problem) and they'll reciprocate by giving you value (e.g. a solution to your problem).
																	- Classical Conditioning
																		- In psychology if you always pair a stimulus which has no particular valence (it's neither positive nor negative) with another stimulus which has a positive or negative consequence then the first stimulus will become associated with the second
																		- E.g.
																			- Always ringing a bell and offering food to a dog will cause it to salivate whenever it is offered food in the future
																- How-to:
																	- Give them an irresistible benefit
																		- Choose the benefit - use a combination of these ideas:
																			- Find out their primary goal
																				- If you offer a thirsty person a glass of water, they'll be happy because drinking something is their primary goal.
																				- If you offer a thirsty person a map with directions to a shop which sells water they'll be far less enthused. It's a secondary goal as it's more indirect.
																				- Think about why a person does everything they do, what is their ultimate purpose.
																			- Know their strengths and weaknesses
																				- Find out what they're good at and what they're not good at.
																				- Don't do or give them something they already can do by themselves at little cost.
																				- Give them something that they can't easily achieve or get by themselves and they'll see it as irresistible.
																			- Know the difference between what they want and what they really need
																				- For example a lazy person may want to just watch TV all day, but they really need to exercise.
																				- This is an example of the two goal types in conflict
																					- A primary goal may be to be healthy (long-term benefit, short-term cost)
																					  What they really need
																					- Whilst a secondary goal is often procrastination (short-term benefit, long-term cost)
																					  What they want
																				- You have to give a person what they perceive they want AND give them what they really need.
																					- E.g. You could offer the lazy person a really easy exercise that even a lazy person could do, like walking on a treadmill which has a TV attached to it.
																			- The solution to an urgent pain
																			- The satisfaction of an unfulfilled desire
																		- Once chosen, enhance the benefit:
																			- Describe vividly what you're offering and how it will impact their lives
																			- Compare why what you're offering is better than all other options they currently have
																	- Make it time-constrained
																		- _Examples_
																			- "My boss has authorised me to buy it today if you accept at this price."
																	- Address any objections before they even arise
																		- Address every possible objection, concern, fear, doubt, excuse, question openly (preferably with evidence e.g. a website or another person's opinion)
														- External communications:
															- First email correspondence
																- Introduction
																	- Hello X,
																	- I am Y from Z.
																- Body:
																	- Benefit Examples
																		- Everyone wants to know WIIFT (What's In It For Them?)
																		- Get to the point and demonstrate why they should care about your email.
																		- _Examples_
																			- I'd like to enquire about your X (product, service)
																			- I'm interested in making a purchase
																			- I've heard all about X, and I think you are Y (compliment)
																			- I know that you are X and I especially liked A, B, C (compliment with specific examples)
																- End:
																	- If making a request or asking a question, end with "Thanks in advance" or "Thank you for your assistance"
																	- Otherwise end with "Best regards" or "Kind regards"
																	- Replying to queries
																		- We hope that this mail has helped you and feel free to contact us again if you have any more questions.

												- How to Research Anything
													- _Overview_
														- Description
															- This will teach you how to perform research on any topic and turn your gathered information into a simple clear report.
														- Benefit
															- You will be able to understand topics quickly without feeling overwhelmed and be able easily teach your learned information to others
													- **SOP**
														- First create a new bullet for your notes and Summary Report
															- _Template: Y's Research on X_
																- Notes
																	-
																- Summary Report
																	-
														- _Searching_
															- Which search site to use
																- In most cases you'll just begin your search using http://www.google.com
																- In specific cases you may start your search from elsewhere:
																	- International physical products
																		- http://alibaba.com
																		- http://aliexpress.com
																	- Videos
																	  http://www.youtube.com
																	- Health
																		- http://www.marksdailyapple.com
																		- https://www.bulletproof.com/
																	- Academic
																		- http://www.sciencedirect.com
																		- http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed
																		- http://scholar.google.com
															- Basic Google Research
																- Choosing the search keyword:
																	- Define the topic in one relevant sentence e.g. 'The impact of coastal pollution in Britain'
																	- Split this sentence into concepts, discarding words which merely describe the relationship between one concept and another e.g. 'Coastal Pollution Britain'
																	- Think of the most important synonyms (words with similar meaning) for each word e.g. instead of 'Britain' you can write 'United Kingdom', 'England' or 'UK'
																- Finding relevant results:
																	- Type in your 'keyword' into the search box and click enter
																	- Read the titles and descriptions of the search results on the first page to see how relevant the search results are to the information you're looking for
																	- On your mouse use the middle button or right-click + 'open in new tab' to open multiple tabs from the first page of your Google search results
																	- Change your search keyword if none of the results are what you're looking for e.g. swap words with synonyms,  remove or add words to get more relevant results etc
																	- Use filters to find the specific content type
																	  https://support.google.com/websearch/answer/142143?hl=en&ref_topic=3180167
																	- Look through webpages and search results for other relevant keywords eg author names, books, concepts, etc
															- Advanced Google Research
																- How to quickly retrieve the most relevant results.
																- Google Search Operators
																	- These are symbols or phrases you can add to your keyword search in order to retrieve more relevant search results.
																	- Essential
																	  AND, OR, NOT, brackets, phrases
																		- AND
																			- Use AND if you want to retrieve references that contain both of the terms you are using. This can narrow a search to find fewer, more relevant, references.
																			- eg Pollution AND Oil spills
																		- OR
																			- Use OR if you want to retrieve references that contain either one of the terms you are using, or both terms together.
																			- This can broaden a search to find more references.
																			- eg Pollution OR Oil spills
																		- NOT
																			- Use NOT if you want to exclude references that contain a particular term. Use with caution!
																			- eg Pollution NOT Oil spills
																			- Related: - (minus)
																		- Brackets
																			- Also, many databases allow you to use brackets to make more complicated searches, for example:
																			- (Pollution OR Oil) AND Seabirds.
																			- The brackets indicate the order that the combining of terms is made in.
																		- Phrases
																			- Many databases allow you to search for phrases, ie a number of words in a sequence together, for example in Library Search and Google you can search for phrases in this way:
																			- "Oil pollution"
																			- The quotation marks indicate that this should be searched for as a phrase, rather than as two words anywhere in the record.
																	- Basic
																		- https://support.google.com/websearch/answer/2466433?hl=en&rd=2
																	- Extended
																		- http://websearch.about.com/library/cheatsheet/blgooglecheatsheet.htm
																	- Comprehensive
																		- http://jwebnet.net/advancedgooglesearch.html
																- How to use Advanced Search
																	- http://www.wikihow.com/Use-Google-Advanced-Search-Tricks
																- Power Searching (cheat sheet)
																	- http://www.powersearchingwithgoogle.com/course/ps/assets/PowerSearchingQuickReference.pdf
														- _Creating the Summary Report_
															- Copy notes down of all relevant information as you research
															- If there are multiple choices to choose from, compare at least 3 options
																- i.e. three types of product we can choose, different suppliers, websites, methods etc
																- Don't choose too many that it becomes overwhelming, not too few to make a good choice either
																- How to compare:
																	- Possibly use a spreadsheet to compare the different qualities against each other if there are many factors
																	- Comparison Criteria Possibilities
																		- Advantages and Disadvantages of each
																		- Money and time cost
															- Simplify your findings
																- i.e. How to transform your discovered information into a clear and easy to understand report.
																- Make the report as short as possible without leaving out critical relevant information
																- Only insert web links if essential and explain links properly
																	- What/why
																		- Limit the amount of web links to non-essential information
																		- (especially when it's lots of words on a page but the critical information may be only a sentence or two.)
																		- Instead copy those few important sentences from the web page into the Doc.
																		- After that you might also add the web link if you feel it is helpful say "And click here for more info" before linking to it.
																	- Example
																		- Wrong
																			- "There are many products we can choose from, just look at this link http://reallylongpageofproducts.com"
																		- Correct
																			- "We can choose from these two products, X is cheaper but takes at least an hour longer to setup whilst Y is more expensive but only takes a total 15 minutes to setup. If you want to learn more see http://reallylongpageofproducts.com and scroll down to the section with the keyword 'Pros and Cons of the Choices' as a heading"
																- _Formatting and visual aids_
																	- - Use bullet points
																	- Use bold, underlining and italics where relevant to highlight important information
																	- Add tables, pictures and other visual aids
																	- If you're comparing multiple options/features, then use a table or spreadsheet
																- Summarise information at the end and detail your recommendations on how to proceed + why
																- If there's is a lot of written information + multimedia then share the Doc with whoever assigned you the task
												- How to Be More Productive
													- Limit Context Switching
													  AKA Singletasking / Avoiding multitasking
														- It's impossible to do two intellectual tasks at once - which is why multitasking doesn't exist, and what you're really doing is rapidly switching focus from one task to another and back again. This is known as context switching.
														- Context switching results in reduced productivity overall so it's best to limit this in any way we can
														- Habits
															- Let other team members know when you're busy focusing on a task and will have to focus on other tasks later
															- Where possible ensure that every individual has a defined role and primarily engages in tasks related to their unique role
															- Don't encourage others to frequently context switch if possible
													- Every task has only one Direct Responsible Individual
														- Steve Jobs’ deeply ingrained concept of a Direct Responsible Individual. Having a “DRI” is a simple, yet highly effective way to ensure accountability for task execution and to alleviate communication issues by clearly showing who is in charge of what. As reported by CNN, “there’s no excuse for Apple employees to have any confusion after a meeting because each to-do on an Apple action list is assigned to a specific DRI.”
														- Psychologically, as shown in this Department of Health study conducted in the UK, people feel more accountable for their actions when they are in a formally designated role (even more so with a boss as formidable as Jobs!).
														  source:: http://www.rcpsych.ac.uk/pdf/Responsibility%20and%20Accountability%20Moving%20on%20for%20New%20Ways%20of%20Working%20to%20a%20Creative,%20Capable%20Workforce.pdf
														- In any group setting, relying on a strong DRI strategy can make an enormous difference in team productivity. For example, DRIs are responsible for:
															- Solving complex issues: the DRI can easily delegate complicated subtasks to the correct people, and he/she can oversee and lead those tasks’ execution thanks to their overall expertise.
															- Initiating new projects or tasks: when the project’s DRI is assigned, that person divides the project into subprojects allowing their colleagues to trust that he/she will delegate the various tasks to the most adept people.
															- Fostering healthy and effective teamwork: having clearly delineated work responsibilities removes uncertainties from people’s to-do list, allowing them to better collaborate with colleagues regarding the tasks and projects at hand.
															- Delivering on actionable items: with a DRI, there are no doubts about who has the main responsibility which therefore expedites the delivery process.
							- Added to Google Drive shared folder
							- Setup Asana

								- [06/07, 09:30] Aaron: Btw regarding the CEO role: there always needs to be someone who has a final say on things in case there is eg a dispute to resolve, and is especially needed when you have this many partners in a business. I put myself down for the role because I'm experienced with acting in this decision-making position in several businesses now and I think you guys are happy with the direction I've put forward so far. If someone else wants to be in this role however then feel free to state your case so others can vote on it, otherwise we'll proceed like this
								- [06/07, 09:32] Aaron: All of the roles on the chart have only a short sentence about what the role entails currently - over time the goal is to create detailed SOPs for each repetitive activity you do within that role (so we can later delegate that role to a new staff member)
								- **FIRST DRAFT **Organisation Chart
									- _Assigned roles_
										- **Aaron**
											- Chief Executive Officer (CEO)
											  Responsible for key decision-making, settling disputes, coordinating with other directors, setting policy and growth
											- IT Director
											  Responsible for managing digital infrastructure and operations
											- App Project Manager
											  Responsible for managing App Developer(s) and coordinating with UX Manager
										- **Adam**
											- Networking Director
											  Responsible for networking with clients (hotel brands) and potential venture partners
											- User Experience (UX) Manager
											  Responsible for enhancing app UX to increase customer lifetime value, user engagement and satisfaction
										- **Daniel**
											- Financial Director
											  Responsible for financial planning, recording and data analysis
											- Service Enhancement Manager
											  Responsible for enhancing the service we can offer e.g. research into hotel operations, their limitations, capabilities, financial goals, staff psychological profiles etc
										- **Evan**
											- Customer Relationship Management Director
											  Responsible for managing our company's relationships with potential and existing customers (hotel customers)
											- Promotional Manager
											  Responsible for research, execution and analysis of promotional campaigns

											  Role is subset of CEO | Manage all staff and training
											- Chief Operations Officer (COO)
											  Role is subset of CEO | Manage day-to-day operations
											- Chief Visionary Officer (CVO)
											  Role is subset of CEO | AKA classic entrepreneur | Responsible for idea generation, research & development etc

											  Role is subset of Networking Manager | Manage one or more contracts with hotel brands
											- Systems Administrator
											  Role is subset of IT Manager | Manage servers and ensure high-availability (up-time)

											  Continuous integration and deployment

													- NOW and LATER buttons
														- Two circles in upper third centered (like Uber) with 'NOW' and 'LATER' on them
															- DEFAULT AVAILABILITY
																- Hides all hotel markers on the map which haven't got a vacancy right now and will still have at least one vacancy for at least one hour from now
															- NOW
																- Prompt saying - how many hours
															- LATER
																- Clicking LATER replaces the NOW+LATER buttons with dropdown menus
																	- Left dropdown menu selector is opened and shows date (DAY DD/MM e.g. Thu 21st Jan)
																	- Right dropdown menu selector is opened and shows time (HH:MM am/pm e.g. 2:00pm) in 1 hour increments
																	- 2nd left dropdown menu for length of stay e.g. 1 hour, 2 hours
																	- At the bottom of the screen is a 'FIND ROOMS' button which submits the search

														- Underneath the address the booking requirements are in italics, partly transparent and smaller text
															- e.g. Minimum booking: 2 hours. Recommended: 4 hours.
														- Picture of room (swipable gallery)

											- _Web app for administration_
												- This is connected to the same database
												- Ability to turn on 'maintenance mode' on the app
													- Why
														- This allows for transactions to be prevented in case the database needs troubleshooting or needs to be rolled to an earlier backup point.
													- When turned on then when users open the app they get a popup with customised text e.g. "Sorry! We're currently undergoing scheduled maintenance. We'll back soon."
													- Then after clicking 'Okay' on the message the app is closed
												- Ability to force app update
													- Why
														- It's problematic when users run old versions of an app because the database schema may change which makes app updating cause errors
													- One column with a text field, placeholder text is 'Which app version should be forced to update?'
													- On the right side of the text field is a green + button, clicking that adds a new text field below
													- Save button at the bottom of the page centered which saves this value to the database
												- Ability to automatically generate invoices for hoteliers
													- Calculated using hotels_1 and contracts_1 database tables

									  collapsed:: true
									  https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/0B41C2IUlybOmMmJmOXduNTBrRHM
										- LATEROOMS:
											- Layout; (1) Map location with surrounding hotels, (2) List of Hotels (sort by: suggested/radius etc), (3) Hotel details (kept simple; pictures, map, pricing options and reviews), (4) Hotel details (more info) - description, check-in/out info, facilities, choose room, (5) Payment window - ability to save hotels as favourites, save card details for future payments.
											- Looks like the initial two views can be changed e.g. bottom left button gives option of MAP VIEW or LIST VIEW.
											- No more than 5 major wireframes. not including a simple booking information wireframe.
											- This is more of the conventional layout for last minute hotel booking hotel apps.
										- HRS SMARTHOTEL:
											- Layout (1) Starts by filtering the hotel room first as a first step e.g early check-in, late-checkout, parking, balcony. (2) Hotel list (sort by) - Heavily focused on mobile specials and deals (something to think about - regarding focus on hourly deals). (3) Literally a wireframe based on deals, (4) Hotel search with dates (5) Hotel info , facilities etc
											- Starting with filters may not be a good shout when thinking about the dynamic of an hourly hotel app. It could be an option but I think starting the wireframes with list or map views would be stronger. I think because the stays would be short etc the need for filtering hotels wouldn't be the primary points for initially using the app. Focus should be on the ease and quickness of booking an hour or so slot. Also gathering the data of hotel facilities may be a bit troublesome in the initial stages of development such as gathering info on balcony, parking…
											- This design may be the weakest of the bunch but included it because of their interesting take on filtering as a strong decision point when booking.
											- Also, their focus on deals may be an important factor when considering the focus of our features. In all honesty I’m booking hotels, apartments and hostels every 3-4 days here and i would primarily book something if it said (40-50% off). I just feel like i should be paying more so i just book it. e.g. in Tulum we were going to stay at a popping young vibe place for £130 a night, but then right underneath on the list we saw a 5 star ALL inclusive for £220 a night (down from £400). So we literally booked it because we thought we’d be missing out on saving almost £200, even though the original was better (in terms of vibe etc.)
										- GROUPON:
											- Obviously Groupon is a bit of oddball but the reason for this selection is the simplicity of the design. In particular, the nearby wireframe that lets you switch from list view to map view. It seems more like a clear view when booking. The travel wireframe is very simple and I can imagine a similar view with ours that includes just the name, hourly price and distance.
											- Groupon was put here primarily for aesthetics and simplicity.
										- JAPANESE LOVE HOTEL:
											- Couldn’t leave this list without including some sort of teller vibe app.
											- Layout (1) list view of hotel, (2), Hotel wireframe that consists of mostly high Q pictures of the hotel with a mapping icon on the bottom left to easily see the hotel in map view. (3)Calendar - Day view. interesting view as we are concentrating on hourly slots. (4) Expanded view of hotel pictures. (5) Map view.
											- The calendar view was interesting to see because it slightly shifts focus on how we present the view of hourly booking. Do we show a calendar that goes into another wireframe with hourly slots? Or do we scrap that and try to minimise wireframes at all cost. **Food for thought**
											- This app seems like it is heavily focused on visual representation of the hotel. It has a minimalistic approach only including some info and focusing on pictures to sell the room.
											- I like the idea of having the map icon on most wireframes as you can always revisit the location without having to go back in the process.
										- Overview:
											- Most hotel apps and booking apps have the same layout of wireframes just in different order. I was trying to look for varied approaches when considering good design.
											- I am a big fan of having the initial wireframe flipped between list view and map view as it seems like the strongest approach when considering short stays. Conversely, one of the initial ideas of having a ‘tinder’ like swiping approach also seemed fresh. Obviously I didn’t see any booking apps with that approach and layout so it may be something to strongly consider if we are to stand out and be different.
											- Maybe the initial wireframe can toggle from map view to swipe view, as opposed to map view and list view. This is something to definitely discuss a long the lines of development.
											- When considering a good design, I think a simple and minimalistic approach would be best suited. The dynamic of booking a hotel on a last minute decision, in my eyes, leans towards making a decision as fast as possible without considering too many variables. e.g. facilities etc. Maybe it would be a good idea to strip down the design much like the Japanese app and keep the wireframes to a minimum.
										- *thats supposed to say "No more than 5 major wireframes. not including a simple booking CONFIRMATION wireframe.

								- Products

										- Monthly subscription for tokens = can be redeemed for different hotels at different amount of tokens
											- Essentially based on what profit we make from each hotel
											- Non-rolling over
											- Discount for first month to entice subscriptions
											- Earn tokens through normal stays, referrals, reviews
												- Very little compared to an actual subscription
									- Hotel Membership packages
										- Gift loyal customers to a particular hotel a discount or free stay at that hotel (if done/redeemed in certain time period)
										- Analytics on AREAS, not competitors
											- World (cheapest), country, region
											- Not on individual competitors so that no new hoteliers are dissuaded from joining the platform and giving away valuable competitive intelligence

								- Marketing Research
									- average tenancy rate
									- average cost per room
										- Average price per room in London is £141 and is expected to reach £142 in 2017 (PWC, 2017)
										- Average price per room in Regional hotel is currently £68 (2016) and could hit £70 in 2017
									- Industry research / statistics
										- UK occupancy rate for April 2017 currently sitting at 70% (VisitEngland, 2017)
										- in London 81% occupancy rate for 2016 - 80% occupancy predicted for 2017 (PWC, 2017)
										- 625,000 rooms available across the UK in 2016 with a projected 18,000 more rooms to be added in 2017 (PWC, 2017)
										- For 2017 in London, it is expected to see RevPAR (Revenue per available room) growth of 3.3% taking RevPar to £120 (PWC, 2017-2018)
											- How is RevPAR calculated?
												- RevPAR = Hotel's average daily room rate (ADR) x Occupancy rate
												- RevPAR= Hotel's total room revenue / total number of rooms available in the period being measured
										- For 2018 a further 2.5% revenue per available room will increase to £123, supported by a 2% average daily rate gain, increasing ADR to £149 (PWC,2017-2018)
										- Hotels and the Sharing Economy
										  Peer-to-peer accommodation is now one of the most established sharing economy sectors - it is estimated that commerce generated across platforms such as Airbnb could rise to nearly £30bn by 2025. **We can help Hoteliers evolve with the times and stay competitive. **

											- Decks Do's & Don'ts
												- Pitch Deck Do’s
													- -Do include this wording at the bottom left of the pitch deck cover page: “Confidential and Proprietary. Copyright (c) by [Name of Company]. All Rights Reserved.”
													- -Do convince the viewer of why the market opportunity is large.
													- -Do include visually interesting graphics and images.
													- -Do send the pitch deck in a PDF format to Hoteliers in advance of a meeting. -Don’t force them to get it from Google Docs, Dropbox, or some other online service, as you are just putting up a barrier to potential clients actually reading it.
													- -Do plan to have a demo of our product as part of the in-person presentation.
													- -Do tell a compelling, memorable, and interesting story that shows your passion for the business.
													- -Do show that you have more than just an idea, and that you have gotten early traction on developing the product, getting other customers etc.
													- -Do have a soundbite for investors to remember you by.
													- -Do use a consistent font size, color, and header title style throughout the slides.
												- Pitch Deck Don’ts
													- -Don’t make the pitch deck more than 15-20 slides long (potential clients have limited attention spans). If you feel you need to add more information, include it as an appendix.
													- -Don’t have too many wordy slides.
													- -Don’t provide excessive financial details, as that can be provided in a follow-up.
													- -Don’t try to cover everything in the pitch deck. Your in-person presentation will give you an opportunity to add and highlight key information.
													- -Don’t use a lot of jargon or acronyms that the potential clients may not immediately understand.
													- -Don’t underestimate or belittle the competition.
													- -Don’t have your pitch deck look out of date. You don’t want a date on the cover page that is several months old. And you don’t want information or metrics in the deck about our business that look stale or outdated.
													- -Don’t have a poor layout, bad graphics, or a low-quality “look and feel.” Think about hiring a graphic designer to give our pitch desk a more professional look.
											- Deck structure (up for debate)
												- Company Overview
												  summarize in 4-6 bullet points your business, what problem it solves, where you are located, the experience of the management team, and any key traction already established.
												- Mission/Vision of the Company
												  In this slide, you want a crisp summary of the mission/vision of the company. Some examples of a mission include:
												  
												  "Our mission is to help hoteliers evolve with the times"
												  
												  Think of this slide as your 15-second compelling elevator pitch.
												- The Team
												  “The Team” slide will typically include:
												  -Pictures of the key team members
												  -Titles of the team members
												  -Short summary of prior employment of the team showing domain experience and relevant expertise
												  -Advisors, consultants, and Board members (sometimes included in this slide to bolster credibility)
												- The Problem
												  You need to define the problem or need your startup is solving, including:
												  
												  -How big is the problem?
												  -Why is it important?
												  -Who are you solving the problem for?
												- The Solution
												  Since the prior slide articulated the problem, “The Solution” section of your investor pitch deck should articulate your proposed solution and why it’s better than other solutions in the market. This deck should be carefully coordinated with the “Product” slide of the pitch deck, as there may be some overlap.
												- The Market Opportunity
												  Investors want to invest in big opportunities with large addressable markets. 
												  On your “Market Opportunity” slide you want to:
												  
												  -Define the market you are in.
												  -Set forth the dollar market size.
												  -Include graphs showing that your company will be addressing a large part of the addressable market.
												- The Product
												  “The Product” slide of the pitch deck should answer:
												  
												  -What are the key features of the product?
												  -Why do users care about the product?
												  -What are the major product milestones?
												  -What are the key differentiated features of the product?
												  -What additional product features are planned?
												  
												  Images, visuals, and videos can play an important role here—don’t just have lengthy written explanations.
												- The Customers
												  If the company has early customers, a “Customers” slide can be powerful and add credibility. Normally, the logos of customers that are well known are included in this slide page.
												- The Technology
												  This slide of the investor pitch deck can address:
												  
												  -The basic technology backbone
												  -Key intellectual property rights the company has (patents, patents pending, copyrights, trademarks, domain names)
												  -Why the technology is or will be superior
												  -Why it will be difficult for a competitor to replicate the technology
												- The Competition
												  Your “Competition” slide should anticipate the following questions:
												  
												  -Who are the company’s competitors?
												  -What gives your company a competitive advantage?
												  -What are the key differentiating features from your competitors?
												  
												  You really have to show an understanding of the competitive landscape and be prepared to answer questions about your competitors. If you don’t understand your competitors, then the potential clients may conclude that you really don’t understand the market.
												- Traction
												  A company that has obtained early traction in some way will be viewed positively. A “Traction” slide is sometimes, but not always, included in the pitch deck (sometimes the company’s progress/traction is just sprinkled through other slides). The “Traction” slide can cover the following:
												  
												  -What early traction has the company gotten (sales, traffic to the company’s website, app downloads, growth metrics, etc., as relevant)?
												  -What strategic partnerships have been consummated?
												  -How can the early traction be accelerated?
												  -Press and accolades
												  -Testimonials
											- Example decks
